break the ice.meaning: to initiate conversation in an awkward social situation
break the ice.meaning_ja: 場の雰囲気を和らげる、打ち解ける
break the ice.explanation: Dates back to the era of trade ships that would need icebreaker vessels to clear paths. Now means easing tension in a social setting.
examples: He told a joke to break the ice at the meeting.
Playing a game is a great way to break the ice at parties.

piece of cake.meaning_ja: 朝飯前、とても簡単なこと
piece of cake.explanation: Originated in the 1930s. Cake is pleasant and effortless to eat, so something easy became a 'piece of cake.'
examples: The exam was a piece of cake.
Don't worry, fixing this will be a piece of cake.

hit the nail on the head.meaning: to be exactly right about something
hit the nail on the head.meaning_ja: 的を射る、まさにその通り
hit the nail on the head.explanation: From carpentry—hitting a nail precisely on its head drives it in correctly. Means identifying something accurately.
examples: You hit the nail on the head with that analysis.
She really hit the nail on the head when she described the problem.

under the weather.meaning_ja: 体調が悪い、気分がすぐれない
under the weather.explanation: From sailing—when sailors felt seasick, they would go below deck to escape the weather. Now means feeling unwell.
examples: I'm feeling a bit under the weather today.
He stayed home because he was under the weather.

break a leg.explanation: Theater superstition holds that wishing someone good luck is bad luck, so performers say 'break a leg' instead.
examples: Break a leg at your audition tomorrow!
You're going to do great—break a leg!

once in a blue moon.meaning_ja: めったにない、ごくまれに
once in a blue moon.explanation: A blue moon is a rare astronomical event (second full moon in a calendar month). Used to describe extreme rarity.
examples: He only visits once in a blue moon.
Once in a blue moon, we get a day off on a weekday.

cost an arm and a leg.meaning_ja: 法外に高い、目が飛び出るほど高い
cost an arm and a leg.explanation: Implies something is so expensive it's as if you'd have to give up your limbs to pay for it.
examples: That designer bag costs an arm and a leg.
Repairs to the car cost an arm and a leg.

let the cat out of the bag.meaning_ja: 秘密をうっかり漏らす
let the cat out of the bag.explanation: Possibly from medieval markets where sellers would substitute a cat for a piglet; opening the bag revealed the fraud.
examples: Don't let the cat out of the bag about the surprise party.
She let the cat out of the bag when she mentioned the promotion.

beat around the bush.meaning: to avoid talking about the main topic directly
beat around the bush.meaning_ja: 遠回しに言う、回りくどい
beat around the bush.explanation: From hunting—beaters would hit around bushes to flush out birds rather than going directly into the bush.
examples: Stop beating around the bush and tell me what happened.
He kept beating around the bush instead of answering the question.

bite the bullet.meaning: to endure a painful or difficult situation bravely
bite the bullet.meaning_ja: 歯を食いしばって耐える、覚悟を決める
bite the bullet.explanation: From battlefield surgery before anesthesia—patients would bite on a bullet to cope with pain.
examples: I decided to bite the bullet and ask for a raise.
We just have to bite the bullet and get through this deadline.

burn the midnight oil.meaning: to work or study late into the night
burn the midnight oil.meaning_ja: 夜遅くまで働く・勉強する
burn the midnight oil.explanation: Before electricity, working late meant burning oil lamps past midnight.
examples: She's been burning the midnight oil to finish the project.
Students often burn the midnight oil before exams.

call it a day.meaning: to stop working; to decide that enough has been done
call it a day.meaning_ja: 今日はここまでにする、切り上げる
call it a day.explanation: Declaring the working day over. Used when you decide to stop an activity for the time being.
examples: We've done enough—let's call it a day.
After three hours of painting, I called it a day.

get out of hand.meaning_ja: 手に負えなくなる、収拾がつかなくなる
get out of hand.explanation: Originally referred to a horse that a rider could no longer control by hand. Now means any situation spiraling out of control.
examples: The party got out of hand when too many people showed up.
Costs are getting out of hand on this project.

go the extra mile.meaning: to make more effort than is expected
go the extra mile.meaning_ja: 期待以上の努力をする
go the extra mile.explanation: Biblical origin from the Sermon on the Mount. Means putting in additional effort beyond what is required.
examples: She always goes the extra mile for her clients.
If you want to succeed, you need to go the extra mile.

in the same boat.meaning: in the same difficult situation as others
in the same boat.meaning_ja: 同じ境遇にある、同じ立場
in the same boat.explanation: If you're in the same boat, you face the same conditions and dangers as everyone else aboard.
examples: We're all in the same boat when it comes to the new regulations.
Don't worry—everyone in the class is in the same boat.

keep an eye on.meaning_ja: 目を光らせる、注意して見る
keep an eye on.explanation: Literally keeping your eye directed at something to ensure it stays safe or behaves properly.
examples: Can you keep an eye on my bag while I'm gone?
Keep an eye on the stock prices this week.

miss the boat.meaning: to miss an opportunity; to be too late
miss the boat.meaning_ja: チャンスを逃す、機会を逸する
miss the boat.explanation: If you miss the boat, it's already departed and you can't get on. Means failing to act in time.
examples: If you don't apply soon, you'll miss the boat.
We missed the boat on that investment opportunity.

pull someone's leg.meaning: to joke with someone; to tease playfully
pull someone's leg.explanation: Origin debated, but means teasing someone in a good-natured way by telling them something untrue.
examples: Are you pulling my leg, or did that really happen?
He loves pulling people's legs with ridiculous stories.

see eye to eye.meaning_ja: 意見が一致する、同じ考えである
see eye to eye.explanation: When two people literally look each other in the eye at the same level, they are on equal footing and in agreement.
examples: We don't always see eye to eye on politics.
The two managers finally see eye to eye on the budget.

spill the beans.explanation: Possibly from ancient Greek voting with beans—spilling them revealed the vote count prematurely.
examples: Come on, spill the beans! What's the surprise?
Someone spilled the beans about the merger.

take it easy.meaning_ja: のんびりする、落ち着いて
take it easy.explanation: A common expression encouraging someone to relax or not overexert themselves.
examples: Take it easy this weekend—you've earned it.
Take it easy, there's no rush.

the last straw.meaning: the final problem in a series that makes the situation unbearable
the last straw.meaning_ja: 最後の一撃、堪忍袋の緒が切れる原因
the last straw.explanation: From 'the straw that broke the camel's back'—one final small burden that causes total collapse.
examples: Being late again was the last straw for his boss.
That rude comment was the last straw—I'm done.

tip of the iceberg.meaning: a small visible part of a much larger problem
tip of the iceberg.explanation: Only about 10% of an iceberg is visible above water. The phrase means a problem is much bigger than it appears.
examples: These complaints are just the tip of the iceberg.
The accounting errors we found were only the tip of the iceberg.

on the fly.explanation: Originally from aviation—making repairs while in flight. Now means doing something quickly without planning.
examples: We had to make changes on the fly.
She can cook on the fly with whatever's in the fridge.

back to square one.meaning: back to the beginning after a failed attempt
back to square one.explanation: Possibly from board games or early BBC football commentary using a numbered grid. Means starting over.
examples: The experiment failed, so it's back to square one.
After the deal fell through, we were back to square one.

ballpark figure.explanation: From baseball—a ballpark is a large area, so a ballpark figure is one that's in the general range but not precise.
examples: Can you give me a ballpark figure for the project cost?
As a ballpark figure, we're looking at about $50,000.

bottom line.meaning: the most important factor; the final result
bottom line.meaning_ja: 最終的な結論、肝心なこと
bottom line.explanation: From accounting—the bottom line of a financial statement shows net profit or loss. Now means the essential point.
examples: The bottom line is that we need more funding.
What's the bottom line on this quarter's sales?

bring to the table.explanation: Imagining a meeting table where everyone brings their contributions. Means offering skills or ideas.
examples: What do you bring to the table as a candidate?
Each team member brings something unique to the table.

cut corners.meaning: to do something in the cheapest or easiest way, often sacrificing quality
cut corners.explanation: From literally cutting corners on a path to save time, but risking a worse result.
examples: They cut corners on construction and the building has problems.
Don't cut corners on safety procedures.

get the ball rolling.meaning: to start a process or activity
get the ball rolling.meaning_ja: 物事を始める、軌道に乗せる
get the ball rolling.explanation: Once a ball starts rolling, it gains momentum. Means initiating something so it can proceed.
examples: Let's get the ball rolling on this project.
Who wants to get the ball rolling with the first presentation?

in a nutshell.meaning: in the fewest possible words; briefly summarized
in a nutshell.meaning_ja: 一言で言えば、要するに
in a nutshell.explanation: From the idea of fitting something into a space as small as a nutshell. Means giving a concise summary.
examples: In a nutshell, the plan is to expand overseas.
That's the situation in a nutshell.

keep someone in the loop.meaning: to keep someone informed about developments
keep someone in the loop.meaning_ja: 情報を共有し続ける、逐一知らせる
keep someone in the loop.explanation: Being 'in the loop' means being part of the information chain. Keeping someone there means updating them regularly.
examples: Please keep me in the loop about any changes.
Make sure to keep the client in the loop.

on the same page.meaning: in agreement; having the same understanding
on the same page.meaning_ja: 同じ認識を持っている、意見が一致している
on the same page.explanation: Like everyone reading from the same page of a document. Means having a shared understanding.
examples: Let's make sure we're all on the same page before we proceed.
Are we on the same page about the deadline?

push the envelope.meaning: to go beyond established limits; to innovate
push the envelope.meaning_ja: 限界に挑戦する、常識を超える
push the envelope.explanation: From aviation—the flight envelope defines safe operating limits. Pushing it means testing those boundaries.
examples: This company constantly pushes the envelope with new technology.
Artists who push the envelope often face criticism at first.

raise the bar.meaning_ja: 基準を上げる、ハードルを高くする
raise the bar.explanation: From high jump and pole vault—literally raising the bar means requiring a higher jump to succeed.
examples: Her performance raised the bar for the entire team.
We need to raise the bar on customer service.

step up to the plate.meaning: to take responsibility; to take action when needed
step up to the plate.meaning_ja: 責任を引き受ける、立ち上がる
step up to the plate.explanation: From baseball—stepping up to home plate to take your turn at bat. Means volunteering or accepting a challenge.
examples: It's time for someone to step up to the plate and lead this project.
When no one else would, she stepped up to the plate.

think outside the box.meaning: to think creatively; to approach problems in new ways
think outside the box.meaning_ja: 型にはまらない発想をする、枠を超えて考える
think outside the box.explanation: From a puzzle involving nine dots that requires drawing lines outside the box to solve. Means creative problem-solving.
examples: We need to think outside the box to solve this problem.
The best innovators think outside the box.

touch base.meaning: to briefly make contact with someone
touch base.meaning_ja: 連絡を取る、軽く打ち合わせをする
touch base.explanation: From baseball—runners must touch each base. In business, it means briefly checking in with someone.
examples: Let's touch base next week about the proposal.
I just wanted to touch base with you on the project status.

up to speed.meaning: fully informed; at the expected level of knowledge or performance
up to speed.meaning_ja: 最新の状況を把握している、追いついている
up to speed.explanation: Like a machine reaching its operating speed. Means someone has all the current information they need.
examples: It took a few weeks to get up to speed at my new job.
Let me bring you up to speed on recent developments.

win-win situation.meaning: a situation where all parties benefit
win-win situation.meaning_ja: 双方にとって有利な状況
win-win situation.explanation: From game theory and negotiation. A win-win means no one loses—everyone gains something.
examples: This partnership is a win-win situation for both companies.
We should look for a win-win situation in these negotiations.

blow off steam.meaning: to release built-up anger or stress
blow off steam.meaning_ja: ストレスを発散する、憂さを晴らす
blow off steam.explanation: From steam engines that must release excess pressure to avoid explosion. Means relieving emotional pressure.
examples: I went for a run to blow off steam after the argument.
Everyone needs to blow off steam sometimes.

butterflies in my stomach.meaning: a nervous, fluttery feeling in the stomach
butterflies in my stomach.meaning_ja: 緊張でそわそわする、胸がドキドキする
butterflies in my stomach.explanation: The sensation of anxiety or excitement can feel like fluttering butterflies in the abdomen.
examples: I had butterflies in my stomach before the interview.
She always gets butterflies in her stomach before performing.

cold feet.meaning: nervousness or hesitation about a decision
cold feet.explanation: Possibly military in origin—soldiers with cold feet couldn't march into battle. Now means losing nerve before a big decision.
examples: He got cold feet and cancelled the wedding.
Don't get cold feet now—we've come too far.

cry over spilt milk.meaning: to be upset about something that cannot be undone
cry over spilt milk.meaning_ja: 覆水盆に返らず、済んだことを嘆く
cry over spilt milk.explanation: Once milk is spilled, it cannot be put back. Means there's no point being upset about irreversible events.
examples: There's no use crying over spilt milk—let's focus on what's next.
What's done is done. No point crying over spilt milk.

down in the dumps.meaning_ja: 落ち込んでいる、意気消沈している
down in the dumps.explanation: 'Dumps' is an old word for a state of melancholy or depression.
examples: She's been down in the dumps since she lost her job.
Don't be down in the dumps—things will get better.

feel blue.meaning_ja: 悲しい気持ちになる、憂鬱になる
feel blue.explanation: Blue has been associated with sadness since the 1300s. Possibly from sailors' custom of flying blue flags when a captain died.
examples: I've been feeling blue all week.
Rainy days make me feel blue.

fly off the handle.explanation: From an axe head flying off its handle when swung—dangerous and uncontrolled. Means losing temper suddenly.
examples: He flew off the handle when he heard the news.
Try not to fly off the handle over small things.

get cold feet.meaning: to become too nervous to proceed with something planned
get cold feet.explanation: Same origin as 'cold feet.' Emphasizes the moment of losing courage before an important event.
examples: She got cold feet about the skydiving trip.
Many people get cold feet before public speaking.

heart of gold.meaning: a very kind and generous nature
heart of gold.explanation: Gold symbolizes something precious and valuable. A heart of gold means someone whose kindness is their most valuable quality.
examples: My grandmother has a heart of gold.
Despite his tough exterior, he has a heart of gold.

keep a stiff upper lip.meaning: to remain brave and unemotional in difficult situations
keep a stiff upper lip.meaning_ja: 感情を表に出さない、毅然としている
keep a stiff upper lip.explanation: A trembling lip shows emotion. Keeping it stiff means maintaining composure. Strongly associated with British culture.
examples: The British are known for keeping a stiff upper lip.
She kept a stiff upper lip during the funeral.

lose your cool.meaning: to lose your temper; to become agitated
lose your cool.meaning_ja: 冷静さを失う、取り乱す
lose your cool.explanation: 'Cool' means composure. Losing it means becoming emotional or angry.
examples: He lost his cool during the argument.
Try not to lose your cool in the negotiation.

on cloud nine.meaning_ja: 天にも昇る気持ち、有頂天
on cloud nine.explanation: Possibly from the US Weather Bureau classification where cloud nine (cumulonimbus) is the highest cloud type.
examples: She's been on cloud nine since she got the promotion.
Winning the award put him on cloud nine.

over the moon.explanation: From the nursery rhyme 'the cow jumped over the moon.' Means being so happy you could jump that high.
examples: They were over the moon when they found out about the baby.
I'm over the moon about the test results.

sick and tired.meaning_ja: うんざりしている、もう嫌だ
sick and tired.explanation: A double emphasis on being exhausted by something. Means having absolutely no more patience for something.
examples: I'm sick and tired of these delays.
She's sick and tired of making excuses for him.

wear your heart on your sleeve.meaning_ja: 感情をあらわにする、心の内を隠さない
wear your heart on your sleeve.explanation: Possibly from medieval jousting where knights wore a lady's favor on their sleeve, showing their affection publicly.
examples: She wears her heart on her sleeve—you always know how she feels.
It's brave to wear your heart on your sleeve.

against the clock.meaning: in a great hurry; with very little time remaining
against the clock.explanation: Racing against the clock rather than competitors. Means working under extreme time pressure.
examples: They were working against the clock to meet the deadline.
It was a race against the clock to save the patient.

at the eleventh hour.meaning: at the very last possible moment
at the eleventh hour.meaning_ja: 土壇場で、ぎりぎりのところで
at the eleventh hour.explanation: From the Bible (Matthew 20:9)—workers hired at the eleventh hour of a twelve-hour day. Means the very last minute.
examples: The agreement was reached at the eleventh hour.
At the eleventh hour, they found a solution.

better late than never.meaning: it's better to do something late than not do it at all
better late than never.meaning_ja: 遅くてもやらないよりはまし
better late than never.explanation: A proverb dating back to Chaucer. Encourages action even when it's overdue.
examples: You finally finished the report? Better late than never.
He started exercising at 60—better late than never.

day in day out.meaning: every single day; continuously and monotonously
day in day out.meaning_ja: 来る日も来る日も、毎日毎日
day in day out.explanation: Emphasizes the repetitive and relentless nature of something that happens every day without change.
examples: She works at the same desk day in day out.
Day in day out, the routine never changes.

from time to time.explanation: A straightforward expression meaning at irregular intervals, not constantly but not rarely either.
examples: From time to time, I like to visit the countryside.
We meet up from time to time for coffee.

in the nick of time.meaning: just barely in time; at the very last moment
in the nick of time.meaning_ja: 間一髪で、ぎりぎり間に合って
in the nick of time.explanation: 'Nick' once meant a precise notch or mark. Arriving at that exact point means cutting it extremely close.
examples: The firefighters arrived in the nick of time.
We caught the train in the nick of time.

kill time.meaning: to do something unimportant while waiting
kill time.meaning_ja: 時間をつぶす、暇つぶしをする
kill time.explanation: To 'kill' time is to make it pass faster by occupying yourself with something trivial.
examples: I was just killing time at the bookstore.
We played cards to kill time before the flight.

once in a while.explanation: A simple idiom meaning at infrequent but somewhat regular intervals.
examples: Once in a while, I treat myself to a fancy dinner.
It's okay to take a break once in a while.

time flies.meaning: time seems to pass very quickly
time flies.meaning_ja: 時が経つのは早い、光陰矢の如し
time flies.explanation: From the Latin 'tempus fugit.' Often said when you realize a long period has passed without noticing.
examples: Time flies when you're having fun.
I can't believe it's December already—time flies!

around the clock.meaning_ja: 24時間休みなく、昼夜を問わず
around the clock.explanation: The hands of a clock go all the way around in 24 hours. Means nonstop, without interruption.
examples: Nurses work around the clock at the hospital.
The factory operates around the clock.

break the bank.meaning: to be very expensive; to use up all one's money
break the bank.meaning_ja: 大金を使う、財布を空にする
break the bank.explanation: From gambling—winning so much that the casino (bank) runs out of money. Now often used negatively to mean something is too expensive.
examples: A vacation doesn't have to break the bank.
That new phone will break the bank.

cash cow.meaning: a business or product that generates consistent, reliable profit
cash cow.meaning_ja: ドル箱、安定して利益を生むもの
cash cow.explanation: Like a cow that reliably produces milk, a cash cow reliably produces income with little additional investment.
examples: The software subscription service is the company's cash cow.
That product line has been a cash cow for years.

foot the bill.meaning: to pay for something, especially a large expense
foot the bill.meaning_ja: 支払いを負担する、勘定を持つ
foot the bill.explanation: 'Foot' means to add up figures at the foot (bottom) of a bill. Now means taking responsibility for payment.
examples: My parents footed the bill for the wedding.
Who's going to foot the bill for the repairs?

make ends meet.meaning: to earn just enough money to cover basic expenses
make ends meet.meaning_ja: 何とか生計を立てる、やりくりする
make ends meet.explanation: The 'ends' are income and expenses—making them meet means having just enough income to cover costs.
examples: It's hard to make ends meet on a single income.
Many families struggle to make ends meet.

penny for your thoughts.meaning: a way of asking someone what they're thinking about
penny for your thoughts.explanation: An old English expression offering a small coin in exchange for hearing someone's thoughts. A gentle way to ask what's on someone's mind.
examples: You look deep in thought—penny for your thoughts?
A penny for your thoughts? You've been quiet all evening.

rain check.meaning: a polite way to decline now but accept later
rain check.meaning_ja: また今度にする、延期する
rain check.explanation: From baseball—when a game was rained out, fans got a 'rain check' ticket for a future game.
examples: Can I take a rain check on dinner? I'm not feeling well.
I'll have to take a rain check—maybe next week?

worth its weight in gold.meaning_ja: 非常に価値がある、金の価値がある
worth its weight in gold.explanation: Gold is one of the most valuable materials by weight. If something is worth its weight in gold, it's extremely precious.
examples: A good mentor is worth their weight in gold.
That advice was worth its weight in gold.

break the news.meaning: to tell someone about something important, often bad
break the news.meaning_ja: 知らせを伝える（特に悪い知らせ）
break the news.explanation: 'Breaking' suggests delivering something sudden or impactful. Usually refers to sharing significant or unwelcome information.
examples: I had to break the news to her about the layoffs.
Who's going to break the news to the team?

get to the point.meaning: to say what is important directly without unnecessary details
get to the point.meaning_ja: 要点を言う、本題に入る
get to the point.explanation: The 'point' is the main idea. Getting to it means skipping preamble and stating the essential information.
examples: Please get to the point—I don't have much time.
He spent twenty minutes talking before finally getting to the point.

give someone the cold shoulder.meaning: to deliberately ignore or be unfriendly to someone
give someone the cold shoulder.meaning_ja: 冷たい態度をとる、無視する
give someone the cold shoulder.explanation: Possibly from serving cold shoulder of meat to unwelcome guests. Means treating someone with deliberate coldness.
examples: She gave him the cold shoulder after their argument.
I don't know why he's giving me the cold shoulder.

hear it through the grapevine.meaning: to learn about something through informal gossip or rumors
hear it through the grapevine.meaning_ja: 人づてに聞く、噂で聞く
hear it through the grapevine.explanation: From the Civil War era when telegraph wires strung through trees resembled grapevines. Informal information spread this way.
examples: I heard through the grapevine that you're moving.
Word got around through the grapevine.

read between the lines.meaning: to understand a hidden meaning that is not directly stated
read between the lines.meaning_ja: 行間を読む、言外の意味を汲み取る
read between the lines.explanation: As if a secret message were written between the visible lines of text. Means understanding implied meaning.
examples: If you read between the lines, you can tell she's unhappy.
Politicians speak carefully, so you need to read between the lines.

speak of the devil.meaning: said when someone you were just talking about appears
speak of the devil.meaning_ja: 噂をすれば影、噂の人が来た
speak of the devil.explanation: Short for 'speak of the devil and he shall appear.' An old superstition that speaking the devil's name would summon him.
examples: We were just talking about you—speak of the devil!
Speak of the devil, here comes Tom now.

the elephant in the room.meaning: an obvious problem that everyone is ignoring
the elephant in the room.meaning_ja: 誰もが気づいているのに触れない問題
the elephant in the room.explanation: An elephant in a room would be impossible to overlook, yet people pretend it's not there. Means an uncomfortable truth no one addresses.
examples: The budget deficit is the elephant in the room.
Let's address the elephant in the room—our sales are dropping.

word of mouth.meaning: information passed by people talking to each other
word of mouth.explanation: Information that spreads through spoken conversation rather than written media or advertising.
examples: The restaurant became popular through word of mouth.
Word of mouth is still one of the best forms of advertising.

back to the drawing board.meaning: to start over because a plan has failed
back to the drawing board.explanation: Designers and architects literally return to their drawing boards to redesign after a failure.
examples: The design didn't work, so it's back to the drawing board.
Our proposal was rejected—back to the drawing board.

blessing in disguise.meaning: something bad that turns out to have good results
blessing in disguise.meaning_ja: 不幸中の幸い、禍を転じて福となす
blessing in disguise.explanation: A good thing that initially appears negative—the blessing is disguised as misfortune.
examples: Losing that job was a blessing in disguise—I found a better one.
The delay was a blessing in disguise because it gave us time to prepare.

burn bridges.meaning: to destroy relationships or eliminate the possibility of returning
burn bridges.meaning_ja: 退路を断つ、関係を壊す
burn bridges.explanation: From military strategy—burning bridges behind you prevents retreat. Means damaging relationships irreparably.
examples: Don't burn bridges with your old company.
He burned bridges when he left without notice.

cross that bridge when you come to it.meaning: to deal with a problem only when it actually arises
cross that bridge when you come to it.meaning_ja: その時になったら考える、取り越し苦労はしない
cross that bridge when you come to it.explanation: No need to worry about crossing a bridge until you reach it. Means not worrying prematurely.
examples: We'll cross that bridge when we come to it.
Don't worry about the exam yet—cross that bridge when you come to it.

hit rock bottom.meaning: to reach the lowest possible point
hit rock bottom.explanation: Rock bottom is the solid foundation beneath everything. Hitting it means you cannot sink any lower.
examples: After losing everything, he hit rock bottom.
The stock price hit rock bottom before recovering.

light at the end of the tunnel.meaning: a sign of hope after a difficult period
light at the end of the tunnel.meaning_ja: トンネルの先の光、希望の兆し
light at the end of the tunnel.explanation: When you're in a dark tunnel, seeing light ahead means you're approaching the exit. Means hope that a difficult time is ending.
examples: After months of treatment, there's finally light at the end of the tunnel.
We can see the light at the end of the tunnel with this project.

make or break.meaning: a situation that will lead to either success or failure
make or break.meaning_ja: 成功か失敗かの分かれ目、のるかそるか
make or break.explanation: The outcome will either make (create success for) or break (destroy) someone or something.
examples: This presentation is make or break for the deal.
The next quarter will be make or break for the startup.

turn over a new leaf.meaning: to start behaving in a better way
turn over a new leaf.meaning_ja: 心を入れ替える、改心する
turn over a new leaf.explanation: 'Leaf' refers to a page in a book. Turning over a new leaf means starting a fresh, clean page in your life.
examples: After his arrest, he turned over a new leaf.
New Year is a good time to turn over a new leaf.

a dime a dozen.meaning: very common and therefore not valuable
a dime a dozen.meaning_ja: ありふれた、掃いて捨てるほどある
a dime a dozen.explanation: If you can buy twelve for a dime (10 cents), each one has very little individual value.
examples: Ideas are a dime a dozen—execution is what matters.
Coffee shops are a dime a dozen in this neighborhood.

add fuel to the fire.meaning: to make a bad situation worse
add fuel to the fire.explanation: Literally adding fuel to flames makes them burn hotter. Means making an already bad situation even worse.
examples: His sarcastic comment only added fuel to the fire.
Don't add fuel to the fire by bringing up old arguments.

add insult to injury.meaning: to make a bad situation even worse by also being disrespectful
add insult to injury.meaning_ja: 泣きっ面に蜂、傷口に塩を塗る
add insult to injury.explanation: Not only is someone harmed, but they are also humiliated. The combination makes things doubly painful.
examples: To add insult to injury, they didn't even apologize.
He was fired, and to add insult to injury, his replacement was his intern.

all ears.meaning_ja: 一心に聞いている、耳をそばだてている
all ears.explanation: As if your entire body were made of ears, giving complete attention to what someone is saying.
examples: Tell me what happened—I'm all ears.
When she started her story, we were all ears.

at the drop of a hat.explanation: From the old custom of dropping a hat as a signal to start a fight or race. Means doing something instantly.
examples: She'd travel anywhere at the drop of a hat.
He gets angry at the drop of a hat.

back against the wall.meaning: in a desperate situation with no easy options
back against the wall.meaning_ja: 追い詰められている、窮地に立たされている
back against the wall.explanation: With your back against a wall, you cannot retreat. Means being in a position where you must fight or take action.
examples: With our backs against the wall, we had to innovate.
The company had its back against the wall during the recession.

bark up the wrong tree.meaning: to pursue a mistaken approach; to blame the wrong person
bark up the wrong tree.meaning_ja: 見当違いをする、お門違いである
bark up the wrong tree.explanation: From hunting dogs that bark at a tree the prey has already left. Means directing efforts in the wrong direction.
examples: If you think I'm responsible, you're barking up the wrong tree.
The investigators were barking up the wrong tree for months.

bend over backwards.meaning: to make an extraordinary effort to help or please someone
bend over backwards.meaning_ja: 全力を尽くす、無理をしてまで助ける
bend over backwards.explanation: Bending over backwards is physically difficult and uncomfortable. Doing it for someone shows extreme effort.
examples: She bends over backwards to accommodate her clients.
We bent over backwards to make them feel welcome.

between a rock and a hard place.meaning_ja: 板挟みになっている、進退きわまった
between a rock and a hard place.explanation: Trapped between two unyielding surfaces with no good option. Means having to choose between two bad alternatives.
examples: I'm between a rock and a hard place—neither option is good.
The manager was caught between a rock and a hard place.

big picture.meaning: the overall situation rather than individual details
big picture.explanation: Stepping back to see the full image rather than focusing on small parts. Means considering the broader context.
examples: Let's not get lost in details—look at the big picture.
You need to see the big picture before making a decision.

bite off more than you can chew.meaning: to take on more responsibility than you can handle
bite off more than you can chew.meaning_ja: 手に余ることをする、力量以上のことを引き受ける
bite off more than you can chew.explanation: Taking a bite of food too large to chew comfortably. Means overcommitting yourself.
examples: I bit off more than I could chew with three projects at once.
Be careful not to bite off more than you can chew.

blow it.meaning: to ruin an opportunity through carelessness or mistakes
blow it.meaning_ja: 台無しにする、しくじる
blow it.explanation: To completely waste or ruin a chance. Informal way of saying you failed when you shouldn't have.
examples: I had a chance to impress the boss and I blew it.
Don't blow it—this is your last chance.

break new ground.meaning: to do something innovative; to pioneer
break new ground.meaning_ja: 新境地を開く、革新的なことをする
break new ground.explanation: From construction—breaking ground means starting to dig for a new building. Now means doing something never done before.
examples: This research breaks new ground in cancer treatment.
The company broke new ground with its approach to remote work.

by the skin of your teeth.meaning: by a very narrow margin; barely
by the skin of your teeth.explanation: From the Bible (Job 19:20). Teeth don't have skin, so 'by the skin of your teeth' means by the thinnest possible margin.
examples: We passed the inspection by the skin of our teeth.
He escaped by the skin of his teeth.

can of worms.meaning: a complicated problem that is difficult to resolve once started
can of worms.meaning_ja: やっかいな問題、パンドラの箱
can of worms.explanation: Opening a can of worms releases many writhing problems that are hard to put back. Means creating complicated issues.
examples: Investigating that would open a whole can of worms.
Let's not open that can of worms right now.

caught red-handed.meaning: caught in the act of doing something wrong
caught red-handed.explanation: Originally referred to being caught with blood on your hands after a crime. Means being caught while committing an offense.
examples: The thief was caught red-handed.
He was caught red-handed eating the last cookie.

change of heart.meaning: a reversal of one's feelings or decision
change of heart.explanation: The heart is seen as the seat of emotions. A change of heart means your feelings or opinions have shifted.
examples: She had a change of heart and decided to stay.
After thinking it over, he had a change of heart.

chip on your shoulder.meaning: an attitude of resentment or grievance
chip on your shoulder.meaning_ja: 肩にのしかかる不満、反感を抱いている
chip on your shoulder.explanation: From the old practice of placing a wood chip on your shoulder and daring someone to knock it off. Means carrying a grudge.
examples: He's always had a chip on his shoulder about not going to college.
Don't go in with a chip on your shoulder.

clear the air.meaning_ja: わだかまりを解消する、雰囲気を一掃する
clear the air.explanation: Like opening windows to let fresh air in after a storm. Means having an honest conversation to resolve tension.
examples: We need to clear the air about what happened yesterday.
A frank discussion helped clear the air.

come to grips with.meaning: to begin to understand and deal with something difficult
come to grips with.meaning_ja: 受け入れて対処する、向き合う
come to grips with.explanation: From wrestling—gripping your opponent means engaging with them directly. Means facing and dealing with a challenge.
examples: She's still coming to grips with the loss.
You need to come to grips with the reality of the situation.

couch potato.meaning: a lazy person who spends a lot of time sitting and watching TV
couch potato.meaning_ja: ごろごろしている人、テレビばかり見ている人
couch potato.explanation: Someone who sits on the couch like a potato—inactive and inert. Coined in the 1970s.
examples: Don't be a couch potato—go outside and play.
He turns into a couch potato every weekend.

cup of tea.meaning: something one enjoys or does well (usually negative: 'not my cup of tea')
cup of tea.meaning_ja: 好みのもの（否定形で使うことが多い）
cup of tea.explanation: British in origin—tea is a personal preference. 'Not my cup of tea' is a polite way to say you don't enjoy something.
examples: Horror movies aren't my cup of tea.
Classical music is really my cup of tea.

cut to the chase.meaning: to get to the important point without wasting time
cut to the chase.meaning_ja: 前置きなしに本題に入る
cut to the chase.explanation: From filmmaking—cutting directly to the exciting chase scene. Means skipping unnecessary preamble.
examples: Let me cut to the chase—we're over budget.
Can we cut to the chase? I have a meeting in ten minutes.

dead end.meaning: a situation with no further possibilities or progress
dead end.meaning_ja: 行き止まり、これ以上進めない状況
dead end.explanation: A road with no exit. Metaphorically means a path that leads nowhere productive.
examples: The investigation reached a dead end.
This career feels like a dead end.

devil's advocate.meaning: someone who argues against a position for the sake of debate
devil's advocate.meaning_ja: あえて反対意見を述べる人
devil's advocate.explanation: From the Catholic Church's process of canonization, where someone would argue against sainthood. Means presenting counterarguments to test an idea.
examples: Let me play devil's advocate for a moment.
She's always the devil's advocate in our meetings.

do a double take.meaning: to look at something again in surprise
do a double take.meaning_ja: 二度見する、驚いて見返す
do a double take.explanation: Looking once, then looking again because you couldn't believe what you saw the first time.
examples: I did a double take when I saw the price.
She did a double take when she recognized her old friend.

draw the line.meaning: to set a limit on what you will accept
draw the line.meaning_ja: 一線を引く、限度を設ける
draw the line.explanation: Literally drawing a line that should not be crossed. Means establishing a boundary.
examples: I draw the line at working on weekends.
You have to draw the line somewhere.

drop the ball.meaning: to make a mistake; to fail to do something expected
drop the ball.meaning_ja: ミスをする、へまをする
drop the ball.explanation: From sports—dropping the ball loses possession or points. Means failing at a task you were responsible for.
examples: Someone dropped the ball on the catering for the event.
I really dropped the ball by forgetting the meeting.

easier said than done.meaning: something that sounds simple but is actually very difficult
easier said than done.meaning_ja: 言うは易く行うは難し
easier said than done.explanation: Acknowledges that describing a solution is much simpler than actually implementing it.
examples: 'Just stay calm'—easier said than done.
Finding a new job is easier said than done.

eat humble pie.meaning: to admit you were wrong and apologize
eat humble pie.meaning_ja: 自分の過ちを認めて謝る、恥を忍ぶ
eat humble pie.explanation: Originally 'umble pie' made from deer organs (umbles), a lowly food. Now means swallowing your pride.
examples: After his predictions were wrong, he had to eat humble pie.
The CEO ate humble pie and apologized publicly.

face the music.meaning: to accept the unpleasant consequences of your actions
face the music.meaning_ja: 報いを受ける、結果に向き合う
face the music.explanation: Possibly from theater—actors facing the orchestra pit. Or military—facing the drumroll of punishment. Means accepting consequences.
examples: It's time to face the music and admit what happened.
You can't run forever—eventually you have to face the music.

fall through the cracks.meaning: to be overlooked or forgotten in a system
fall through the cracks.meaning_ja: 見落とされる、抜け落ちる
fall through the cracks.explanation: Like something small enough to slip through gaps in a surface. Means being missed or neglected.
examples: Some students fall through the cracks in large schools.
The complaint fell through the cracks and was never addressed.

from scratch.meaning: from the very beginning with no prior preparation
from scratch.explanation: From sports where the 'scratch' was the starting line drawn in the dirt. Starting from scratch means no head start.
examples: She built her business from scratch.
I made the cake from scratch—no box mix.

get off the ground.meaning: to successfully begin or make progress
get off the ground.explanation: Like an airplane taking off. Means getting a project or plan started and moving forward.
examples: We finally got the project off the ground.
It took years to get the business off the ground.

get your act together.meaning: to organize yourself and start behaving properly
get your act together.meaning_ja: しっかりする、態度を改める
get your act together.explanation: From show business—getting your performance act prepared and polished. Means becoming organized and responsible.
examples: You need to get your act together before the review.
He finally got his act together and finished school.

give it a shot.meaning_ja: やってみる、試してみる
give it a shot.explanation: Taking a shot at something—attempting it, even if you might miss. Means making an attempt.
examples: I've never tried sushi, but I'll give it a shot.
Why not give it a shot? You have nothing to lose.

go back on your word.meaning_ja: 約束を破る、前言を撤回する
go back on your word.explanation: To reverse or retract what you previously promised. Means not keeping your commitment.
examples: He went back on his word and sold the house to someone else.
A good leader never goes back on their word.

go down the drain.meaning_ja: 水の泡になる、台無しになる
go down the drain.explanation: Like water or money going down a drain—irretrievably lost. Means efforts or resources being wasted.
examples: All our hard work went down the drain.
Don't let your savings go down the drain.

go with the flow.meaning: to accept things as they happen without trying to control them
go with the flow.meaning_ja: 流れに身を任せる、成り行きに任せる
go with the flow.explanation: Like floating with a river current rather than swimming against it. Means being relaxed and adaptable.
examples: I don't have a plan—I'll just go with the flow.
Sometimes you just have to go with the flow.

good riddance.meaning: expressing relief at being free of someone or something unpleasant
good riddance.meaning_ja: せいせいした、いなくなってよかった
good riddance.explanation: Riddance means the act of getting rid of something. Expresses happiness that something unwanted is gone.
examples: He finally quit? Good riddance!
Good riddance to that old car—it was always breaking down.

hang in there.meaning: to persist during a difficult time; don't give up
hang in there.meaning_ja: 頑張って、持ちこたえて
hang in there.explanation: Like holding on to a rope or branch to avoid falling. An encouraging phrase meaning to keep trying.
examples: Hang in there—things will improve.
The semester is almost over. Just hang in there.

have a lot on your plate.meaning: to have many tasks or responsibilities to deal with
have a lot on your plate.meaning_ja: やることがたくさんある、抱えきれない
have a lot on your plate.explanation: Like a plate piled high with food. Means being burdened with many tasks.
examples: I can't take on more work—I already have a lot on my plate.
She has a lot on her plate with the new baby and her job.

head over heels.meaning_ja: すっかり夢中で、首ったけ
head over heels.explanation: So overwhelmed by love that you're tumbling head over heels. Means being completely infatuated.
examples: He's head over heels for her.
They fell head over heels in love during college.

hit the ground running.meaning: to start something and immediately work hard at it
hit the ground running.meaning_ja: すぐに全力で取り組む、即戦力となる
hit the ground running.explanation: Like jumping from a vehicle and running immediately upon landing. Means starting with full energy and effort.
examples: She hit the ground running in her new role.
We need someone who can hit the ground running.

hit the road.meaning: to leave; to begin a journey
hit the road.explanation: Literally starting to travel on the road. A casual way of saying it's time to leave.
examples: We should hit the road before traffic gets bad.
Let's hit the road—it's a long drive.

hold your horses.meaning: to slow down; to be patient
hold your horses.meaning_ja: ちょっと待って、焦らないで
hold your horses.explanation: From horseback riding—pulling the reins to slow the horses. Means someone should wait and be patient.
examples: Hold your horses—I'm not finished yet.
Hold your horses! We need to think this through.

in hot water.meaning_ja: 困った状況にある、まずいことになっている
in hot water.explanation: Being in hot water is uncomfortable and dangerous. Means being in a problematic situation.
examples: He's in hot water with his boss for missing the deadline.
If you're not careful, you'll end up in hot water.

in over your head.meaning: involved in something too difficult to handle
in over your head.meaning_ja: 手に負えない状態、力不足の状態
in over your head.explanation: Like being in water deeper than your height—you can't touch bottom and may drown. Means being out of your depth.
examples: I realized I was in over my head with this project.
Ask for help before you get in over your head.

it takes two to tango.meaning: both parties are responsible for a situation
it takes two to tango.meaning_ja: 両方に責任がある、喧嘩両成敗
it takes two to tango.explanation: Tango requires two dancers. Means both people involved share responsibility, especially in a conflict.
examples: Don't blame only him—it takes two to tango.
The argument was their fault equally. It takes two to tango.

jump on the bandwagon.meaning: to join others in doing something popular
jump on the bandwagon.explanation: From political campaigns where a bandwagon (music wagon) would attract supporters. Joining it means following the popular trend.
examples: Everyone's jumping on the AI bandwagon.
Don't just jump on the bandwagon—think for yourself.

jump the gun.meaning: to act too soon; to start something prematurely
jump the gun.meaning_ja: フライングする、早まった行動をとる
jump the gun.explanation: From racing—starting before the starting gun fires. Means acting before the appropriate time.
examples: Let's not jump the gun—we need more data.
She jumped the gun and announced the results too early.

keep your chin up.meaning: to stay cheerful and optimistic in a difficult situation
keep your chin up.meaning_ja: くよくよしないで、元気を出して
keep your chin up.explanation: Holding your chin up rather than letting it drop in despair. An encouraging phrase.
examples: Keep your chin up—you'll find a job soon.
Things are tough, but keep your chin up.

kick the bucket.explanation: Origin debated—possibly from standing on a bucket to hang oneself, or from the beam (bucket) that pigs were hung from after slaughter.
examples: He kicked the bucket at the ripe old age of 95.
I want to travel the world before I kick the bucket.

kill two birds with one stone.meaning: to accomplish two things with one action
kill two birds with one stone.explanation: Literally hitting two birds with a single thrown stone. Means achieving two goals simultaneously.
examples: By cycling to work, I kill two birds with one stone—exercise and commuting.
We can kill two birds with one stone by combining the meetings.

know the ropes.meaning: to understand how something works; to be experienced
know the ropes.meaning_ja: コツを知っている、要領を心得ている
know the ropes.explanation: From sailing—knowing which ropes control which sails was essential. Means being familiar with how things work.
examples: It takes a few months to know the ropes at a new job.
She really knows the ropes in this industry.

leave no stone unturned.meaning: to search thoroughly; to try everything possible
leave no stone unturned.meaning_ja: あらゆる手を尽くす、徹底的に調べる
leave no stone unturned.explanation: From an ancient Greek story—turning over every stone to find hidden treasure. Means being extremely thorough.
examples: The police left no stone unturned in the investigation.
We'll leave no stone unturned to find a solution.

let sleeping dogs lie.meaning: to avoid interfering with a situation that might cause problems
let sleeping dogs lie.meaning_ja: 触らぬ神に祟りなし、寝た子を起こすな
let sleeping dogs lie.explanation: Waking a sleeping dog might cause it to bite. Means not disturbing a potentially volatile situation.
examples: Don't bring up the argument again—let sleeping dogs lie.
Sometimes it's best to let sleeping dogs lie.

long story short.meaning: to summarize without giving all the details
long story short.meaning_ja: 手短に言うと、かいつまんで言えば
long story short.explanation: Abbreviation of 'to make a long story short.' Signals you're skipping details to get to the point.
examples: Long story short, we didn't get the contract.
Long story short, she moved to Tokyo and started a new career.

lose track of.meaning: to no longer know where something is or what is happening
lose track of.meaning_ja: 見失う、把握できなくなる
lose track of.explanation: Like a hunter losing the animal's tracks. Means no longer being aware of or monitoring something.
examples: I lost track of time and missed my appointment.
It's easy to lose track of expenses.

make a long story short.meaning: to give a brief summary of something
make a long story short.explanation: Cutting out unnecessary details to deliver the core message quickly.
examples: To make a long story short, the project was cancelled.
I'll make a long story short—we won the case.

miss the mark.meaning: to fail to achieve what was intended
miss the mark.meaning_ja: 的外れである、目標を達成できない
miss the mark.explanation: From archery—missing the target. Means falling short of expectations or goals.
examples: The new product missed the mark with consumers.
His speech missed the mark entirely.

neck of the woods.explanation: Originally American—'neck' referred to a narrow stretch of woods or land. Now means someone's local area.
examples: What brings you to my neck of the woods?
There aren't many restaurants in this neck of the woods.

no strings attached.meaning_ja: 条件なしで、ひもつきでなく
no strings attached.explanation: Like a puppet with no strings—free to move independently. Means an offer with no hidden requirements.
examples: The scholarship comes with no strings attached.
It's a free trial, no strings attached.

not rocket science.meaning_ja: そんなに難しくない、簡単なこと
not rocket science.explanation: Rocket science is considered extremely complex. Saying something isn't rocket science means it's straightforward.
examples: Following the instructions isn't rocket science.
Come on, it's not rocket science—just press the button.

off the top of my head.meaning: from memory without careful thought or research
off the top of my head.meaning_ja: ぱっと思いつくのは、今すぐには
off the top of my head.explanation: As if pulling an answer from the surface of your mind, not from deep thought or research.
examples: Off the top of my head, I'd say there are about twenty.
I can't remember the exact number off the top of my head.

on the fence.meaning_ja: どちらとも決めかねている、迷っている
on the fence.explanation: Sitting on a fence between two fields—not committed to either side. Means being undecided.
examples: I'm still on the fence about whether to accept the offer.
Many voters are still on the fence.

on the tip of my tongue.meaning_ja: 喉まで出かかっている
on the tip of my tongue.explanation: The word or memory feels like it's right there on your tongue but you can't quite get it out.
examples: His name is on the tip of my tongue.
The answer is on the tip of my tongue—give me a second.

on thin ice.meaning: in a risky or precarious situation
on thin ice.meaning_ja: 危うい立場にある、薄氷を踏む思い
on thin ice.explanation: Walking on thin ice risks falling through. Means being in a dangerous position where one more mistake could be catastrophic.
examples: You're on thin ice after that stunt you pulled.
The company is on thin ice financially.

once bitten, twice shy.meaning: after a bad experience, one is more cautious
once bitten, twice shy.meaning_ja: 羹に懲りて膾を吹く、一度失敗すると慎重になる
once bitten, twice shy.explanation: If you've been bitten once, you'll be more careful next time. Means learning caution from past mistakes.
examples: After the last investment loss, I'm cautious—once bitten, twice shy.
She doesn't trust online shopping anymore. Once bitten, twice shy.

out of the blue.explanation: From 'out of the clear blue sky'—like lightning striking on a clear day. Means something completely unexpected.
examples: Out of the blue, she called after ten years.
The announcement came completely out of the blue.

over your head.meaning_ja: 理解を超えている、ちんぷんかんぷん
over your head.explanation: Like something flying over your head—you can't reach or grasp it. Means beyond your comprehension.
examples: The physics lecture was way over my head.
I'm afraid this technical jargon is over my head.

pass the buck.meaning: to shift responsibility to someone else
pass the buck.meaning_ja: 責任を人に押し付ける
pass the buck.explanation: From poker—a buck (marker) was passed to indicate whose turn it was to deal. Passing it means avoiding your turn/responsibility.
examples: Stop passing the buck and take responsibility.
The manager kept passing the buck to junior staff.

pay through the nose.meaning: to pay an extremely high price
pay through the nose.explanation: Possibly from a Viking-era nose tax in Ireland. Means paying far more than something is worth.
examples: We paid through the nose for those concert tickets.
You'll pay through the nose for repairs at that garage.

play it by ear.meaning: to decide what to do as a situation unfolds, without a plan
play it by ear.meaning_ja: 臨機応変にやる、出たとこ勝負で行く
play it by ear.explanation: From music—playing by ear means without sheet music, improvising. Means proceeding without a fixed plan.
examples: I don't have a plan—let's just play it by ear.
We'll play it by ear depending on the weather.

pull strings.meaning: to use influence or connections to get something done
pull strings.meaning_ja: 裏で手を回す、コネを使う
pull strings.explanation: Like a puppeteer pulling strings to control puppets. Means using behind-the-scenes influence.
examples: He pulled some strings to get the job.
Can you pull some strings to get us a reservation?

put all your eggs in one basket.meaning: to risk everything on a single venture
put all your eggs in one basket.meaning_ja: 一つのことに全てを賭ける
put all your eggs in one basket.explanation: If you carry all eggs in one basket and drop it, you lose everything. Means not diversifying your risks.
examples: Don't put all your eggs in one basket—diversify your investments.
Relying on one client is putting all your eggs in one basket.

put your foot down.meaning: to firmly assert your authority or opinion
put your foot down.meaning_ja: 断固として譲らない、毅然とした態度をとる
put your foot down.explanation: Planting your foot firmly shows you won't be moved. Means insisting on something decisively.
examples: She put her foot down and refused to work overtime.
Sometimes you have to put your foot down.

put your foot in your mouth.meaning: to say something embarrassing or inappropriate by mistake
put your foot in your mouth.meaning_ja: 失言する、まずいことを言ってしまう
put your foot in your mouth.explanation: Imagining literally putting your foot in your mouth—an awkward, embarrassing thing to do. Means saying something you regret.
examples: I put my foot in my mouth when I asked about her ex.
He's always putting his foot in his mouth.

raining cats and dogs.explanation: Origin debated—possibly from old thatched roofs where animals would fall through, or from old English 'catadupe' meaning waterfall.
examples: Take an umbrella—it's raining cats and dogs out there.
We couldn't go out because it was raining cats and dogs.

ring a bell.meaning: to sound familiar; to vaguely recall something
ring a bell.meaning_ja: 聞き覚えがある、ピンとくる
ring a bell.explanation: A bell ringing triggers recognition. If something rings a bell, it sparks a vague memory.
examples: Does the name John Smith ring a bell?
That story rings a bell—I think I've heard it before.

rule of thumb.meaning: a practical guideline based on experience rather than exact measurement
rule of thumb.explanation: Possibly from using the thumb as a rough measuring tool. Means a general principle that works in most cases.
examples: As a rule of thumb, save 20% of your income.
A good rule of thumb is to arrive ten minutes early.

run out of steam.meaning_ja: 息切れする、勢いがなくなる
run out of steam.explanation: From steam engines—when they run out of steam, they stop moving. Means losing momentum or energy.
examples: The project ran out of steam after the initial excitement.
I ran out of steam by the afternoon.

save face.meaning: to avoid humiliation or preserve one's reputation
save face.meaning_ja: 面目を保つ、体面を守る
save face.explanation: Originated from Chinese culture. 'Face' represents dignity and respect. Saving it means preserving your reputation.
examples: He resigned quietly to save face.
The compromise allowed both sides to save face.

scratch the surface.meaning: to deal with only a small part of a much larger subject or problem
scratch the surface.meaning_ja: 表面をなぞるだけ、ほんの一部に触れるだけ
scratch the surface.explanation: Only scratching the outer surface, not going deeper. Means barely beginning to explore something.
examples: We've only scratched the surface of what's possible with AI.
This report barely scratches the surface of the issue.

second nature.meaning: something so familiar that it feels instinctive
second nature.meaning_ja: 第二の天性、体に染みついていること
second nature.explanation: So well-practiced that it feels as natural as an inborn trait. Means something done effortlessly from habit.
examples: After years of practice, playing the piano is second nature to her.
Driving became second nature after a few months.

see the light.meaning: to suddenly understand or realize something
see the light.meaning_ja: 悟る、理解する、目が覚める
see the light.explanation: Like a light turning on in a dark room, bringing clarity. Means having a moment of understanding.
examples: After reading the article, I finally saw the light.
It took him years, but he finally saw the light about healthy eating.

shoot the breeze.meaning: to have a casual, relaxed conversation
shoot the breeze.meaning_ja: おしゃべりする、雑談する
shoot the breeze.explanation: Firing at nothing in particular—just chatting aimlessly. Means having a lighthearted conversation.
examples: We sat on the porch and shot the breeze for hours.
I called just to shoot the breeze.

sit on the fence.meaning: to avoid making a decision or taking a side
sit on the fence.meaning_ja: 日和見する、態度を決めかねる
sit on the fence.explanation: Same as 'on the fence'—sitting between two sides without committing to either.
examples: You can't sit on the fence forever—make a choice.
Politicians often sit on the fence on controversial issues.

skeleton in the closet.meaning: a shameful secret from the past
skeleton in the closet.meaning_ja: 人に知られたくない秘密、隠された恥
skeleton in the closet.explanation: A hidden skeleton represents a dark secret that would be shocking if discovered.
examples: Every family has a skeleton in the closet.
The investigation uncovered several skeletons in his closet.

sleep on it.meaning: to wait until the next day before making a decision
sleep on it.meaning_ja: 一晩考える、結論を翌日に持ち越す
sleep on it.explanation: The idea that a good night's sleep helps you think more clearly about a decision.
examples: That's a big decision—why don't you sleep on it?
I slept on it and decided to accept the offer.

slip of the tongue.explanation: Your tongue 'slipped' and said something you didn't intend. A minor spoken error.
examples: I called her by the wrong name—it was just a slip of the tongue.
His comment about the merger was a slip of the tongue.

snake in the grass.explanation: A snake hidden in grass is a concealed danger. Means someone who pretends to be a friend but is secretly hostile.
examples: I trusted him, but he turned out to be a snake in the grass.
Watch out for her—she's a real snake in the grass.

so far so good.meaning: things have gone well up to this point
so far so good.explanation: A report that progress has been positive to date, though the outcome is still uncertain.
examples: The surgery went well—so far so good.
How's the new job? So far so good.

stab someone in the back.meaning: to betray someone who trusts you
stab someone in the back.explanation: Attacking someone from behind when they're not expecting it. Means a treacherous betrayal.
examples: He stabbed me in the back by telling the boss my secret.
She felt stabbed in the back by her closest friend.

steal someone's thunder.meaning: to take credit for or attention from someone else's achievement
steal someone's thunder.meaning_ja: 人の手柄を横取りする、お株を奪う
steal someone's thunder.explanation: From 18th-century theater—a playwright invented a thunder effect that was then copied by a rival production.
examples: She stole my thunder by announcing the results before me.
Don't steal his thunder—let him share the good news himself.

straight from the horse's mouth.meaning: from the original or most reliable source
straight from the horse's mouth.meaning_ja: 確かな筋から、本人から直接
straight from the horse's mouth.explanation: From horse racing—you can tell a horse's age by looking at its teeth (directly from the horse). Means information from the primary source.
examples: I heard it straight from the horse's mouth—the CEO herself.
Get the information straight from the horse's mouth.

take a back seat.meaning: to take a less important role
take a back seat.meaning_ja: 控えめな立場をとる、脇役に回る
take a back seat.explanation: The back seat of a car is not the driver's position. Means stepping back from a leading role.
examples: His career took a back seat when he became a father.
Sometimes you need to take a back seat and let others lead.

take it with a grain of salt.meaning: to not take something too seriously; to be skeptical
take it with a grain of salt.meaning_ja: 話半分に聞く、鵜呑みにしない
take it with a grain of salt.explanation: From Latin 'cum grano salis.' A grain of salt makes food more palatable—similarly, adding skepticism makes dubious information more digestible.
examples: Take online reviews with a grain of salt.
I'd take his predictions with a grain of salt.

take the plunge.meaning: to commit to doing something bold or risky
take the plunge.meaning_ja: 思い切ってやる、決断する
take the plunge.explanation: Like diving into cold water—once you plunge in, there's no going back. Means making a daring commitment.
examples: After years of planning, they took the plunge and opened a restaurant.
Are you ready to take the plunge and move abroad?

the ball is in your court.meaning: it's your turn to make a decision or take action
the ball is in your court.meaning_ja: あなたの番です、決めるのはあなた
the ball is in your court.explanation: From tennis—when the ball is in your court, it's your responsibility to hit it back.
examples: I've made my offer—the ball is in your court.
We've done our part; the ball is in their court now.

the best of both worlds.meaning: a situation where you enjoy two different advantages simultaneously
the best of both worlds.explanation: Having advantages from two different options without the drawbacks of either.
examples: Working from home gives me the best of both worlds.
This hybrid car offers the best of both worlds.

the whole nine yards.meaning: everything; the full extent of something
the whole nine yards.explanation: Origin debated—possibly from fabric, ammunition belts, or cement trucks. Means the complete amount.
examples: They decorated the house—lights, ornaments, the whole nine yards.
We got the full treatment: dinner, dessert, the whole nine yards.

throw in the towel.meaning: to give up; to admit defeat
throw in the towel.meaning_ja: 降参する、ギブアップする
throw in the towel.explanation: From boxing—a trainer throws a towel into the ring to signal their fighter is withdrawing from the match.
examples: After months of trying, he threw in the towel.
Don't throw in the towel yet—we still have a chance.

throw someone under the bus.meaning: to sacrifice someone else to save yourself
throw someone under the bus.meaning_ja: 人を犠牲にして自分を守る、裏切る
throw someone under the bus.explanation: Pushing someone into danger (under a bus) to protect yourself. Means blaming or betraying someone for personal gain.
examples: He threw his colleague under the bus during the investigation.
I can't believe she threw me under the bus like that.

turn a blind eye.meaning: to deliberately ignore something wrong or illegal
turn a blind eye.explanation: From Admiral Nelson, who literally put his telescope to his blind eye to avoid seeing a signal to retreat.
examples: The manager turned a blind eye to the safety violations.
We can't turn a blind eye to this problem.

two-edged sword.meaning: something that has both positive and negative consequences
two-edged sword.explanation: A sword sharp on both edges can cut the enemy but also harm the wielder. Means something with advantages and disadvantages.
examples: Social media is a two-edged sword for businesses.
Fame can be a two-edged sword.

under your belt.explanation: Like food under your belt (eaten and digested). Means something accomplished that adds to your experience.
examples: She has ten years of experience under her belt.
With a few certifications under your belt, you'll be more competitive.

up in the air.meaning_ja: 未定の、まだ決まっていない
up in the air.explanation: Like something tossed up that hasn't landed yet. The outcome is still unknown.
examples: Our vacation plans are still up in the air.
The project timeline is up in the air.

water under the bridge.meaning: past events that are no longer important
water under the bridge.meaning_ja: 過ぎたこと、もう終わったこと
water under the bridge.explanation: Water that has flowed under a bridge is gone and can't be retrieved. Means past events that should be forgiven and forgotten.
examples: We had our differences, but that's all water under the bridge now.
Let's forget about it—it's water under the bridge.

weather the storm.meaning_ja: 嵐を乗り越える、困難を切り抜ける
weather the storm.explanation: From sailing—surviving through a storm at sea. Means enduring and getting through a tough time.
examples: The company weathered the storm of the economic downturn.
We'll weather this storm together.

wet blanket.meaning: a person who spoils other people's fun
wet blanket.meaning_ja: 場の雰囲気を壊す人、興ざめな人
wet blanket.explanation: A wet blanket was used to smother fires. A person who dampens enthusiasm is called a wet blanket.
examples: Don't be a wet blanket—come dance with us!
He's such a wet blanket at parties.

when pigs fly.meaning_ja: ありえないこと、絶対に起こらない
when pigs fly.explanation: Pigs cannot fly, so this phrase expresses that something is impossible.
examples: He'll clean his room when pigs fly.
Sure, I'll lend you money—when pigs fly!

whole new ball game.meaning_ja: まったく別の話、全く違う状況
whole new ball game.explanation: From baseball—a new game means starting fresh with new conditions. Means the situation has fundamentally changed.
examples: With the new regulations, it's a whole new ball game.
When kids are involved, it's a whole new ball game.

wild goose chase.meaning: a hopeless search or pursuit of something unattainable
wild goose chase.explanation: From an old horse-racing game where riders followed a lead horse like geese. Now means a futile pursuit.
examples: Looking for that file turned into a wild goose chase.
The police realized they'd been on a wild goose chase.

wrap your head around.meaning_ja: 理解する、頭で把握する
wrap your head around.explanation: As if physically wrapping your mind around a concept to grasp it. Means coming to terms with something complex.
examples: I can't wrap my head around quantum physics.
It took me a while to wrap my head around the new system.

writing on the wall.meaning: clear signs that something bad is going to happen
writing on the wall.meaning_ja: 不吉な前兆、破滅の兆し
writing on the wall.explanation: From the Bible (Daniel 5)—mysterious writing appeared on a wall predicting the fall of Babylon.
examples: The writing was on the wall for the company months ago.
Can't you see the writing on the wall?

you can say that again.meaning_ja: まったくその通り、本当にそうだ
you can say that again.explanation: Encouraging someone to repeat what they said because you agree so strongly with it.
examples: 'This weather is terrible.' 'You can say that again!'
'The food here is amazing.' 'You can say that again!'

zero in on.meaning: to focus attention directly on something
zero in on.meaning_ja: 焦点を合わせる、的を絞る
zero in on.explanation: From military targeting—zeroing in means adjusting aim to hit exactly on target.
examples: Let's zero in on the main problem.
The detective zeroed in on the key suspect.

ace in the hole.meaning: a hidden advantage or resource kept in reserve
ace in the hole.explanation: From poker—an ace dealt face down (in the hole) is a hidden winning card.
examples: Our ace in the hole is the patent we filed last year.
She always has an ace in the hole during negotiations.

actions speak louder than words.meaning: what you do is more important than what you say
actions speak louder than words.meaning_ja: 行動は言葉よりも雄弁である
actions speak louder than words.explanation: A proverb emphasizing that demonstrating through behavior is more meaningful than making promises.
examples: He says he'll change, but actions speak louder than words.
Don't just promise—actions speak louder than words.

all in the same boat.meaning: everyone facing the same situation together
all in the same boat.meaning_ja: みんな同じ境遇にある
all in the same boat.explanation: Everyone on the same boat faces the same conditions—storms, calm seas, or sinking.
examples: The pay cut affects everyone—we're all in the same boat.
Don't complain to me. We're all in the same boat.

a taste of your own medicine.meaning: receiving the same bad treatment you've given others
a taste of your own medicine.explanation: Being forced to take the unpleasant medicine you've been giving to others. Means experiencing what you've inflicted on others.
examples: After years of bullying others, he finally got a taste of his own medicine.
It's time she got a taste of her own medicine.

back on track.meaning: returning to the correct course after a deviation
back on track.meaning_ja: 軌道に戻る、正常に戻る
back on track.explanation: Like a train returning to its tracks. Means resuming progress after an interruption.
examples: After the setback, we're finally back on track.
Let's get this meeting back on track.

bad apple.meaning: one person who negatively affects the whole group
bad apple.meaning_ja: 腐ったリンゴ、悪影響を及ぼす人
bad apple.explanation: One rotten apple can spoil the whole barrel. Means one problematic person corrupting others.
examples: There's always a bad apple in every team.
One bad apple can ruin the whole department's morale.

beat a dead horse.meaning: to continue discussing or pursuing something that is already finished
beat a dead horse.meaning_ja: もう終わったことをくどくど言う、無駄な努力をする
beat a dead horse.explanation: Hitting a dead horse won't make it move. Means wasting effort on something that won't change.
examples: We've made the decision—stop beating a dead horse.
I know I'm beating a dead horse, but I still think we should reconsider.

behind closed doors.explanation: Activities happening behind doors that are closed to the public. Means done in secret or private.
examples: The negotiations took place behind closed doors.
What happens behind closed doors stays there.

bells and whistles.meaning: extra features or accessories that are attractive but not essential
bells and whistles.meaning_ja: 付加機能、おまけの装飾
bells and whistles.explanation: From trains or organs that had bells and whistles as extras. Means attractive but non-essential additions.
examples: The basic model doesn't have all the bells and whistles.
I don't need all the bells and whistles—just something that works.

bent out of shape.meaning_ja: 怒っている、腹を立てている
bent out of shape.explanation: Something bent out of its proper shape is distorted and not functioning right. Means being emotionally disturbed.
examples: Don't get bent out of shape over a small mistake.
He got all bent out of shape when I criticized his work.

birds of a feather flock together.meaning: people with similar interests or qualities tend to associate
birds of a feather flock together.explanation: Birds of the same species fly together. Means people with similar characteristics naturally group together.
examples: They're both tech enthusiasts—birds of a feather flock together.
It's no surprise they became friends. Birds of a feather flock together.

blood is thicker than water.meaning: family relationships are stronger than other bonds
blood is thicker than water.explanation: Family ties (blood) are more binding than friendships or other connections (water).
examples: He'll always side with his brother—blood is thicker than water.
In the end, blood is thicker than water.

blow someone away.explanation: So impressive it metaphorically blows someone off their feet. Means making a powerful positive impression.
examples: Her performance blew everyone away.
The view from the top blew me away.

bolt from the blue.meaning: a complete surprise; something totally unexpected
bolt from the blue.explanation: Lightning from a clear blue sky. A shocking event with no prior warning.
examples: His resignation was a bolt from the blue.
The news came like a bolt from the blue.

bone to pick.meaning: a complaint or grievance to discuss with someone
bone to pick.meaning_ja: 文句がある、言いたいことがある
bone to pick.explanation: Like a dog with a bone to gnaw on—you have an issue you want to work through with someone.
examples: I have a bone to pick with you about the schedule.
She had a bone to pick with the landlord.

break even.meaning: to neither make nor lose money
break even.meaning_ja: 損益分岐点に達する、トントンになる
break even.explanation: Revenue exactly equals costs—no profit, no loss. The point where gains and losses are balanced.
examples: The business hopes to break even by next year.
We just about broke even on the event.

burn the candle at both ends.meaning: to overwork yourself by staying up late and getting up early
burn the candle at both ends.meaning_ja: 無理をして働きすぎる、体を酷使する
burn the candle at both ends.explanation: A candle burning at both ends uses up twice as fast. Means exhausting yourself through overwork.
examples: She's been burning the candle at both ends studying for exams.
You can't burn the candle at both ends forever.

bury the hatchet.meaning: to make peace; to end a conflict
bury the hatchet.meaning_ja: 和解する、争いをやめる
bury the hatchet.explanation: From Native American tradition of literally burying a weapon to signify peace. Means resolving a dispute.
examples: After years of feuding, they finally buried the hatchet.
Let's bury the hatchet and move on.

by and large.explanation: From sailing—'by' and 'large' referred to different wind directions. Together they mean 'in all conditions' or generally.
examples: By and large, the project was a success.
The food was, by and large, very good.

call the shots.meaning: to be the one making decisions; to be in charge
call the shots.meaning_ja: 指揮を執る、決定権を持つ
call the shots.explanation: From billiards or military—calling your shots means declaring what you intend to do. Means being the decision-maker.
examples: She calls the shots in this department.
Who's calling the shots around here?

catch someone off guard.meaning: to surprise someone when they are not prepared
catch someone off guard.explanation: From fencing or military—catching someone when their guard (defense) is down. Means surprising someone.
examples: The question caught me off guard.
The sudden price increase caught consumers off guard.

comparing apples to oranges.meaning_ja: 全く異なるものを比較する
comparing apples to oranges.explanation: Apples and oranges are both fruits but quite different. Comparing them is not a fair comparison.
examples: Comparing those two teams is like comparing apples to oranges.
You're comparing apples to oranges—the situations are completely different.

cool as a cucumber.meaning: very calm and composed, even in a stressful situation
cool as a cucumber.meaning_ja: 冷静沈着な、泰然自若とした
cool as a cucumber.explanation: Cucumbers are cool to the touch inside. A person who is cool as a cucumber remains unflappable.
examples: Despite the pressure, she was cool as a cucumber.
He walked into the interview cool as a cucumber.

curiosity killed the cat.meaning: being too inquisitive can lead to trouble
curiosity killed the cat.explanation: A warning that being overly curious can have dangerous consequences, like a cat investigating something harmful.
examples: I'd stop asking questions if I were you—curiosity killed the cat.
She couldn't resist looking—curiosity killed the cat.

dark horse.meaning: a competitor about whom little is known but who could do surprisingly well
dark horse.meaning_ja: ダークホース、予想外の強敵
dark horse.explanation: From horse racing—an unknown horse that unexpectedly wins. Means an unexpected contender.
examples: The new candidate is a dark horse in the election.
Watch out for the dark horse in this competition.

dig your own grave.meaning: to do something that will cause your own failure
dig your own grave.meaning_ja: 自分で自分の墓穴を掘る
dig your own grave.explanation: Preparing for your own downfall through your own actions. Means self-destructive behavior.
examples: By lying to the auditors, the company dug its own grave.
He's digging his own grave with those reckless decisions.

don't count your chickens before they ha.meaning: don't assume success before it's confirmed
don't count your chickens before they ha.meaning_ja: 捕らぬ狸の皮算用、早とちりするな
don't count your chickens before they ha.explanation: Eggs might not all hatch into chickens. Means not relying on an uncertain outcome.
examples: The deal isn't signed yet—don't count your chickens before they hatch.
I might get the job, but I won't count my chickens.

don't put all your eggs in one basket.meaning: don't risk everything on one plan or investment
don't put all your eggs in one basket.meaning_ja: 一つに全てを賭けるな
don't put all your eggs in one basket.explanation: Diversify your risks. If the one basket drops, you lose everything.
examples: Invest in different sectors—don't put all your eggs in one basket.
Don't put all your eggs in one basket when applying to colleges.

down to earth.meaning_ja: 地に足がついた、気取らない
down to earth.explanation: Grounded on earth rather than floating in the clouds. Means being realistic and humble.
examples: Despite her fame, she's very down to earth.
I appreciate his down-to-earth approach.

dressed to the nines.meaning: wearing very formal or elegant clothing
dressed to the nines.meaning_ja: めかし込んで、正装して
dressed to the nines.explanation: Origin debated—possibly from the 99th Regiment or from the amount of fabric needed for a formal suit. Means very well dressed.
examples: Everyone was dressed to the nines for the gala.
She was dressed to the nines for the awards ceremony.

drop in the bucket.meaning: a very small amount compared to what is needed
drop in the bucket.meaning_ja: 焼け石に水、微々たるもの
drop in the bucket.explanation: A single drop in a large bucket is negligible. Means an insignificant contribution to a much larger need.
