examples: The donation was just a drop in the bucket compared to the total cost.
My savings are a drop in the bucket for a house down payment.

every cloud has a silver lining.meaning: there is something positive in every bad situation
every cloud has a silver lining.meaning_ja: どんな不幸にも良い面がある
every cloud has a silver lining.explanation: Even dark storm clouds have bright edges where sunlight shines through. Means finding hope in difficult situations.
examples: I lost my job, but every cloud has a silver lining—I found a better one.
Remember, every cloud has a silver lining.

fair and square.meaning: honestly and according to the rules
fair and square.explanation: 'Square' once meant fair or honest. Together with 'fair,' it emphasizes complete honesty.
examples: We won the contract fair and square.
He earned the promotion fair and square.

fall on deaf ears.meaning: to be ignored; for advice or warnings to go unheeded
fall on deaf ears.meaning_ja: 無視される、聞き入れられない
fall on deaf ears.explanation: Words falling on ears that cannot or will not hear them. Means being disregarded.
examples: Our warnings fell on deaf ears.
His pleas for help fell on deaf ears.

feather in your cap.meaning: an achievement to be proud of
feather in your cap.explanation: From the custom of adding a feather to a cap for each enemy defeated. A mark of accomplishment.
examples: Landing that client was a real feather in her cap.
The award is a feather in the university's cap.

few and far between.meaning_ja: めったにない、数少ない
few and far between.explanation: Things that are few in number and spread far apart—meaning very scarce.
examples: Good restaurants in this area are few and far between.
Opportunities like this are few and far between.

fight fire with fire.meaning: to use the same methods as an opponent
fight fire with fire.explanation: From actual firefighting—setting controlled fires to stop a wildfire. Means countering a strategy with the same strategy.
examples: If they're going to play dirty, we'll fight fire with fire.
Sometimes you have to fight fire with fire.

fish out of water.meaning: a person in an unfamiliar and uncomfortable situation
fish out of water.meaning_ja: 場違いな人、居心地の悪い状況にある人
fish out of water.explanation: A fish removed from water is helpless and out of its element. Means feeling out of place.
examples: I felt like a fish out of water at the formal dinner.
He's a fish out of water in the corporate world.

food for thought.meaning_ja: 考える材料、検討に値すること
food for thought.explanation: Ideas that 'feed' the mind, worth digesting and contemplating.
examples: The documentary gave me a lot of food for thought.
That's some food for thought. Let me think about it.

get a grip.meaning: to gain control of your emotions; to calm down
get a grip.meaning_ja: しっかりする、落ち着く
get a grip.explanation: Literally gripping something firmly to maintain control. Means getting your emotions under control.
examples: You need to get a grip and stop panicking.
Get a grip—it's not the end of the world.

get away with murder.meaning: to do something wrong without being punished
get away with murder.meaning_ja: 何をしても許される、罰を受けずに済む
get away with murder.explanation: Even committing the worst crime and escaping punishment. Means avoiding consequences for bad behavior.
examples: That kid gets away with murder because his parents never discipline him.
Some companies get away with murder on environmental regulations.

get down to business.meaning: to start working seriously; to focus on the important matter
get down to business.meaning_ja: 本題に入る、仕事に取りかかる
get down to business.explanation: Moving from casual talk or preparation to the actual work that needs to be done.
examples: Enough small talk—let's get down to business.
We need to get down to business if we want to finish today.

get the hang of.meaning: to learn how to do something; to become skilled at something
get the hang of.meaning_ja: コツをつかむ、やり方がわかる
get the hang of.explanation: Understanding how something 'hangs together' or works. Means becoming comfortable with a new skill.
examples: It took me a while, but I finally got the hang of it.
You'll get the hang of the software after a few days.

give the benefit of the doubt.meaning: to trust someone's word when you're not sure if they're telling the truth
give the benefit of the doubt.meaning_ja: 疑わしきは罰せず、善意に解釈する
give the benefit of the doubt.explanation: When evidence is uncertain, assuming the person is innocent or telling the truth.
examples: I'll give him the benefit of the doubt this time.
Let's give her the benefit of the doubt—she might have a good reason.

go overboard.meaning: to do too much; to be excessive
go overboard.explanation: From falling overboard from a ship—going too far. Means being excessive in some action.
examples: Don't go overboard with the decorations.
She went overboard buying gifts for everyone.

graveyard shift.meaning: a work shift during the late night hours
graveyard shift.explanation: Named because late-night hours are when graveyards are most eerily quiet. Typically midnight to early morning.
examples: He's been working the graveyard shift at the factory.
Nobody wants to do the graveyard shift.

green with envy.meaning_ja: ひどくねたんでいる、嫉妬に狂っている
green with envy.explanation: Shakespeare associated green with jealousy in Othello. Green has been linked to envy ever since.
examples: She was green with envy when she saw my new car.
His success made his rivals green with envy.

have a change of heart.meaning: to change your opinion or attitude about something
have a change of heart.meaning_ja: 心変わりする、考えを変える
have a change of heart.explanation: The heart changes its stance—your feelings shift from one position to another.
examples: She was going to decline but had a change of heart.
I hope the board has a change of heart about the funding.

have the upper hand.meaning: to have an advantage over someone
have the upper hand.meaning_ja: 優位に立っている、有利な立場にある
have the upper hand.explanation: From various games and physical contests where having the higher hand position gives control.
examples: In negotiations, the buyer has the upper hand.
Technology gave us the upper hand over the competition.

hit the books.explanation: To aggressively engage with your textbooks. Means studying with serious effort.
examples: I need to hit the books for the exam.
She's been hitting the books all weekend.

hit the jackpot.meaning: to achieve great success or win a large prize
hit the jackpot.meaning_ja: 大当たりする、大成功する
hit the jackpot.explanation: From gambling—hitting the jackpot means winning the biggest prize. Means achieving an exceptionally good outcome.
examples: She hit the jackpot with her first startup.
We hit the jackpot finding this apartment.

hit the sack.explanation: Old mattresses were large sacks filled with straw. 'Hitting' the sack meant going to bed.
examples: I'm exhausted—time to hit the sack.
We should hit the sack early tonight.

in a pickle.meaning: in a difficult or awkward situation
in a pickle.meaning_ja: 困った状況にある、窮地に陥っている
in a pickle.explanation: Shakespeare used this in The Tempest. Being preserved in vinegar like a pickle implies being stuck in an uncomfortable state.
examples: I'm in a real pickle—I double-booked myself.
The team was in a pickle after losing their star player.

in someone's shoes.meaning: in someone else's situation or position
in someone's shoes.explanation: Imagining wearing another person's shoes to understand their experience. Means empathizing with someone.
examples: If I were in your shoes, I'd take the job.
Try to put yourself in her shoes.

in the dark.meaning_ja: 知らされていない、情報がない
in the dark.explanation: Being in the dark means you can't see what's happening. Means being left without information.
examples: The employees were kept in the dark about the merger.
I'm completely in the dark about what happened.

in the doghouse.meaning: in trouble with someone, especially a partner
in the doghouse.meaning_ja: 嫌われている、機嫌を損ねている
in the doghouse.explanation: Being banished to the doghouse (outside) as punishment. Means being in someone's bad graces.
examples: He's in the doghouse for forgetting their anniversary.
I'm in the doghouse with my boss after that mistake.

in the driver's seat.explanation: The driver controls where the vehicle goes. Being in the driver's seat means being in charge.
examples: After the acquisition, the new CEO is in the driver's seat.
You're in the driver's seat—it's your decision.

in the heat of the moment.meaning: while feeling strong emotions; without thinking rationally
in the heat of the moment.meaning_ja: カッとなって、衝動的に
in the heat of the moment.explanation: The 'heat' of intense emotion overrides rational thought. Means acting impulsively during an emotional peak.
examples: He said things he didn't mean in the heat of the moment.
Decisions made in the heat of the moment are often regretted.

jack of all trades.meaning: a person who is competent at many different things
jack of all trades.explanation: Originally 'jack of all trades, master of none.' Means someone with wide but not deep skills.
examples: He's a jack of all trades—he can fix anything.
In a small company, you need to be a jack of all trades.

keep your eyes peeled.meaning_ja: 目を皿にして見る、注意して見張る
keep your eyes peeled.explanation: Eyelids peeled back wide open for maximum alertness. Means being highly observant.
examples: Keep your eyes peeled for the turnoff.
Keep your eyes peeled for any sales.

last but not least.meaning: the final item is equally important as what came before
last but not least.meaning_ja: 最後になりましたが大事なこととして
last but not least.explanation: A phrase used when introducing the final point to emphasize it's not less important just because it's last.
examples: And last but not least, I'd like to thank our sponsors.
Last but not least, don't forget to save your work.

let your hair down.meaning: to relax and enjoy yourself without worrying
let your hair down.meaning_ja: くつろぐ、リラックスして楽しむ
let your hair down.explanation: From women unpinning their hair at the end of a formal occasion. Means dropping formalities and relaxing.
examples: It's Friday night—let your hair down!
The party was a chance for everyone to let their hair down.

like pulling teeth.meaning_ja: 非常に困難で退屈な、骨の折れる
like pulling teeth.explanation: Extracting teeth is painful and difficult. Used for tasks that require excessive effort.
examples: Getting information from him is like pulling teeth.
The negotiations were like pulling teeth.

look on the bright side.meaning: to focus on the positive aspects of a situation
look on the bright side.meaning_ja: 良い面を見る、前向きに考える
look on the bright side.explanation: Every situation has a bright (positive) side. Encourages optimism despite difficulties.
examples: Look on the bright side—at least you learned something.
I always try to look on the bright side.

move the goalposts.meaning: to change the rules or criteria unfairly
move the goalposts.meaning_ja: 途中でルールを変える、ゴールポストを動かす
move the goalposts.explanation: In football, moving the goalposts makes it harder to score. Means changing expectations after someone has already started working.
examples: They keep moving the goalposts on our team's targets.
It's unfair to move the goalposts after we've agreed on the terms.

nip it in the bud.meaning: to stop something at an early stage before it becomes a bigger problem
nip it in the bud.meaning_ja: 芽のうちに摘む、早期に対処する
nip it in the bud.explanation: From gardening—removing a bud prevents unwanted growth. Means dealing with problems early.
examples: We need to nip this problem in the bud.
If you see bad habits forming, nip them in the bud.

no pain no gain.meaning_ja: 苦労なくして成果なし
no pain no gain.explanation: You must endure difficulty or discomfort to achieve results. Common in fitness but applies broadly.
examples: The workout is tough, but no pain no gain.
Studying is hard, but no pain no gain.

not in a million years.explanation: Emphatic way of saying something will never happen—not even if you wait a million years.
examples: Would I ever skydive? Not in a million years.
Not in a million years did I think I'd win.

off the beaten path.meaning: in a place or way that is not commonly known or visited
off the beaten path.meaning_ja: 人里離れた、あまり知られていない
off the beaten path.explanation: Away from the well-worn path that most people travel. Means unusual or undiscovered.
examples: We found a restaurant off the beaten path.
I prefer vacations off the beaten path.

on a roll.meaning_ja: 好調で、波に乗っている
on a roll.explanation: Like a ball rolling with momentum—each success leads to the next. Means being on a winning streak.
examples: She's scored three goals—she's on a roll!
The company has been on a roll this quarter.

on edge.meaning_ja: 神経がぴりぴりしている、落ち着かない
on edge.explanation: Like balancing on the edge of something—uncomfortable and precarious. Means feeling anxious.
examples: Everyone's been on edge since the layoffs.
I've been on edge all day waiting for the results.

on the back burner.meaning: given low priority; put aside for later
on the back burner.meaning_ja: 後回しにされている、優先度が低い
on the back burner.explanation: On a stove, pots on the back burner simmer slowly while front burners get more attention. Means deprioritized.
examples: The project has been put on the back burner.
I had to put my hobbies on the back burner while I focus on work.

on the ball.meaning: alert, competent, and quick to understand
on the ball.meaning_ja: 気が利く、しっかりしている
on the ball.explanation: From sports—keeping your eye on the ball means being attentive. Means being sharp and capable.
examples: She's really on the ball—nothing gets past her.
You need to be on the ball in this job.

on the bright side.meaning: focusing on the positive aspect of a situation
on the bright side.explanation: Looking at the bright, positive side rather than the dark, negative one.
examples: On the bright side, we learned a lot from the experience.
The weather was bad, but on the bright side, we had fun indoors.

on the dot.explanation: When the clock hand is exactly on the dot of the hour. Means precisely on time.
examples: The meeting starts at 9 on the dot.
She arrived at 3 o'clock on the dot.

on the rocks.meaning: in a state of difficulty or failure (especially relationships)
on the rocks.meaning_ja: うまくいっていない、破綻しかけている
on the rocks.explanation: A ship on the rocks is in serious trouble and may sink. Also means a drink served over ice.
examples: Their marriage has been on the rocks lately.
The business is on the rocks.

out of the woods.meaning_ja: 危機を脱した、困難を切り抜けた
out of the woods.explanation: The woods represent danger (getting lost, wild animals). Being out of them means being safe.
examples: The patient isn't out of the woods yet.
We're not out of the woods, but things are improving.

paint the town red.meaning: to go out and have a wildly enjoyable time
paint the town red.explanation: Origin debated—possibly from a rowdy Marquess who literally painted buildings red. Means going out for a wild night.
examples: It's my birthday—let's paint the town red!
They painted the town red after the big win.

play devil's advocate.meaning: to argue the opposing side for the sake of discussion
play devil's advocate.meaning_ja: あえて反対意見を述べる
play devil's advocate.explanation: Deliberately taking the contrary position to test the strength of an argument or idea.
examples: Let me play devil's advocate—what if the plan fails?
I'm just playing devil's advocate to make sure we've considered everything.

play hardball.meaning: to act aggressively or ruthlessly in negotiations
play hardball.meaning_ja: 強硬な態度をとる、容赦なくやる
play hardball.explanation: Hardball is more aggressive than softball. Means using tough, uncompromising tactics.
examples: The union decided to play hardball in negotiations.
If they won't cooperate, we'll have to play hardball.

play hooky.meaning: to skip school or work without permission
play hooky.explanation: American slang for truancy. Means being absent without a legitimate excuse.
examples: Let's play hooky and go to the beach.
He used to play hooky from school all the time.

point of no return.meaning: the stage at which it's impossible to go back
point of no return.meaning_ja: 引き返せない地点、後戻りできない段階
point of no return.explanation: From aviation—the point where a plane doesn't have enough fuel to return to its origin. Must continue forward.
examples: We've passed the point of no return on this project.
Once you sign the contract, it's the point of no return.

put two and two together.meaning: to figure something out from available evidence
put two and two together.meaning_ja: 状況から推測する、察する
put two and two together.explanation: Like adding 2+2 to get 4—drawing an obvious conclusion from the facts available.
examples: When I saw them together, I put two and two together.
It wasn't hard to put two and two together.

read someone like a book.meaning: to easily understand someone's thoughts or feelings
read someone like a book.meaning_ja: 人の考えを簡単に見抜く
read someone like a book.explanation: Someone whose emotions are as easy to read as the pages of a book. Means being transparent.
examples: She can read me like a book—I can never surprise her.
Don't try to lie to him. He reads people like a book.

rock the boat.meaning: to disturb a stable situation; to cause trouble
rock the boat.meaning_ja: 波風を立てる、事を荒立てる
rock the boat.explanation: Rocking a boat puts everyone in it at risk. Means disrupting a peaceful situation.
examples: He didn't want to rock the boat by asking for changes.
Sometimes you need to rock the boat to make progress.

run the show.meaning: to be in charge; to manage everything
run the show.explanation: Like a director running a show or production. Means having complete control over operations.
examples: Since the manager left, she's been running the show.
Let me know who runs the show around here.

saved by the bell.meaning: rescued from a difficult situation at the last moment
saved by the bell.meaning_ja: ベルに救われる、ぎりぎりで助かる
saved by the bell.explanation: From boxing—the bell ending a round can save a struggling fighter. Means being rescued just in time.
examples: The teacher was about to call on me, but I was saved by the bell.
The meeting ended just in time—saved by the bell.

sell like hotcakes.meaning: to sell very quickly and in large quantities
sell like hotcakes.explanation: Hotcakes (pancakes) at fairs would sell extremely fast because they were warm and delicious.
examples: The new phone is selling like hotcakes.
Tickets for the concert sold like hotcakes.

shape up or ship out.meaning_ja: 態度を改めないと出ていけ
shape up or ship out.explanation: From military/naval origin—either improve your performance or be sent away. An ultimatum.
examples: The boss told him to shape up or ship out.
Shape up or ship out—we can't tolerate this anymore.

show your true colors.meaning: to reveal one's real character or intentions
show your true colors.meaning_ja: 本性を現す、正体を見せる
show your true colors.explanation: From sailing—ships would fly false flags and then show their true colors before attacking. Means revealing who you really are.
examples: Under pressure, people show their true colors.
He finally showed his true colors during the crisis.

small talk.meaning: light, casual conversation about unimportant topics
small talk.explanation: Talk that is 'small' in significance—about weather, news, or other light topics rather than serious matters.
examples: We made small talk while waiting for the meeting to start.
I'm not very good at small talk.

smooth sailing.explanation: Sailing on calm waters with favorable winds. Means everything is going well and easily.
examples: After the initial problems, it's been smooth sailing.
Once you pass the exam, it should be smooth sailing.

stab in the dark.meaning: a wild guess; an attempt without much information
stab in the dark.meaning_ja: 当てずっぽう、やみくもな推測
stab in the dark.explanation: Stabbing in darkness means you can't see your target. Means guessing without enough information.
examples: I'll take a stab in the dark and say it's from the 1990s.
It was a stab in the dark, but my guess turned out to be right.

stand your ground.meaning: to refuse to change your position or give in
stand your ground.meaning_ja: 一歩も引かない、自分の立場を守る
stand your ground.explanation: From military—holding your position despite attack. Means maintaining your stance firmly.
examples: She stood her ground during the negotiation.
Sometimes you have to stand your ground on principle.

start from scratch.meaning: to begin again from the very beginning
start from scratch.explanation: Same origin as 'from scratch'—the scratch line was the starting point in races.
examples: The code was so buggy we had to start from scratch.
After the fire, they had to start from scratch.

take a toll.meaning: to cause damage or suffering gradually
take a toll.meaning_ja: 犠牲を強いる、悪影響を与える
take a toll.explanation: A toll is a fee for crossing a bridge. Figuratively, things take a toll on your health, energy, or resources.
examples: The stress is taking a toll on his health.
Years of neglect took a toll on the building.

take the bull by the horns.meaning: to deal with a difficult situation directly and bravely
take the bull by the horns.meaning_ja: 困難に正面から立ち向かう
take the bull by the horns.explanation: Grabbing a bull by its horns is dangerous but direct. Means confronting challenges head-on.
examples: She took the bull by the horns and confronted her boss.
It's time to take the bull by the horns and solve this problem.

the cream of the crop.meaning: the very best of a group
the cream of the crop.explanation: Cream rises to the top of milk—it's the richest part. The crop's cream is its finest produce.
examples: These students are the cream of the crop.
Only the cream of the crop make it into the program.

the early bird catches the worm.meaning: those who act first get the advantage
the early bird catches the worm.explanation: A bird that wakes up early has the best chance of finding worms. Means being early or proactive gives you an advantage.
examples: I always arrive early—the early bird catches the worm.
If you want the best deals, remember: the early bird catches the worm.

the grass is always greener on the other.meaning: other people's situations always seem better than your own
the grass is always greener on the other.explanation: Your neighbor's lawn looks better from a distance. Means people tend to envy others' situations.
examples: He wants her job, but the grass is always greener on the other side.
Before you quit, remember the grass is always greener on the other side.

the plot thickens.meaning: a situation becomes more complicated or interesting
the plot thickens.meaning_ja: 話が複雑になってきた、事態が面白くなってきた
the plot thickens.explanation: From novels and plays—when the plot thickens, new twists make the story more complex.
examples: New evidence has surfaced—the plot thickens.
She denied it, but now witnesses say otherwise. The plot thickens.

through thick and thin.meaning: through good times and bad; no matter what happens
through thick and thin.meaning_ja: 良い時も悪い時も、どんな時でも
through thick and thin.explanation: Whether the path is thick (dense forest) or thin (clear), you continue regardless. Means unwavering loyalty.
examples: We've been friends through thick and thin.
She supported him through thick and thin.

to each their own.meaning: everyone has their own preferences and that's acceptable
to each their own.meaning_ja: 人それぞれ、好みは人によって違う
to each their own.explanation: Acknowledging that different people like different things, and that's perfectly fine.
examples: I don't like horror movies, but to each their own.
To each their own—I prefer tea over coffee.

tongue-tied.meaning: unable to speak because of nervousness or shyness
tongue-tied.meaning_ja: うまく話せない、緊張で言葉が出ない
tongue-tied.explanation: As if your tongue were literally tied and couldn't move freely to form words.
examples: I was tongue-tied when she spoke to me.
He gets tongue-tied in front of large audiences.

too many cooks spoil the broth.meaning: too many people involved in something can ruin it
too many cooks spoil the broth.meaning_ja: 船頭多くして船山に登る
too many cooks spoil the broth.explanation: If too many cooks add ingredients to one soup, the result is bad. Means too many people managing one task causes problems.
examples: Let's limit the committee—too many cooks spoil the broth.
The project failed because too many cooks spoiled the broth.

twist someone's arm.meaning: to persuade someone to do something they're reluctant to do
twist someone's arm.meaning_ja: 無理やり説得する、腕をねじる
twist someone's arm.explanation: Literally twisting an arm would be painful coercion. Figuratively means strong persuasion.
examples: I didn't want to go, but they twisted my arm.
You don't have to twist my arm—I'd love to come!

under the table.meaning: done secretly, especially involving illegal payments
under the table.explanation: Passing something under a table so others can't see. Means secret or illicit dealings.
examples: They paid him under the table to avoid taxes.
Some workers are paid under the table.

up for grabs.meaning: available for anyone to take or compete for
up for grabs.meaning_ja: 誰でも手に入れられる、争奪戦の状態
up for grabs.explanation: Something thrown up in the air for anyone to grab. Means available to whoever gets there first.
examples: The championship title is still up for grabs.
There are free tickets up for grabs.

walking on eggshells.meaning: being extremely careful not to upset someone
walking on eggshells.meaning_ja: 腫れ物に触るように接する
walking on eggshells.explanation: Eggshells are fragile—walking on them requires extreme care. Means being overly cautious around a sensitive person.
examples: Everyone's walking on eggshells around the boss today.
I feel like I'm walking on eggshells in this relationship.

watch your step.meaning: be careful about your behavior or actions
watch your step.meaning_ja: 気をつけろ、言動に注意しろ
watch your step.explanation: Literally watching where you step to avoid tripping. Figuratively means being careful about what you do or say.
examples: Watch your step—the boss is in a bad mood.
You'd better watch your step around the new regulations.

way over your head.meaning: far too complex for you to understand
way over your head.meaning_ja: 全く理解できないほど難しい
way over your head.explanation: Intensified version of 'over your head'—far beyond your ability to comprehend.
examples: Advanced calculus is way over my head.
The legal jargon was way over my head.

wipe the slate clean.meaning: to start fresh by forgetting past mistakes
wipe the slate clean.meaning_ja: 白紙に戻す、過去を清算する
wipe the slate clean.explanation: In the past, chalk slates were wiped clean to start writing anew. Means removing all previous records of wrongdoing.
examples: Let's wipe the slate clean and start over.
The new year is a chance to wipe the slate clean.

wouldn't hurt a fly.meaning: describes someone who is very gentle and kind
wouldn't hurt a fly.meaning_ja: 蚊も殺せないほど優しい
wouldn't hurt a fly.explanation: Someone so gentle they wouldn't harm even a tiny fly. Means being extremely kind-natured.
examples: He looks tough, but he wouldn't hurt a fly.
She's the sweetest person—she wouldn't hurt a fly.

X marks the spot.explanation: From treasure maps where an X was drawn to indicate where treasure was buried.
examples: Dig here—X marks the spot.
According to the map, X marks the spot right here.

year in year out.meaning: every year without change; continuously year after year
year in year out.explanation: Like 'day in day out' but on a yearly scale. Emphasizes monotonous repetition over years.
examples: Year in year out, the festival draws thousands of visitors.
She does the same thing year in year out.

your guess is as good as mine.meaning: I don't know the answer either
your guess is as good as mine.meaning_ja: 私にもわからない、見当がつかない
your guess is as good as mine.explanation: Neither person knows the answer—both would be equally guessing.
examples: When will this be fixed? Your guess is as good as mine.
I have no idea what happened—your guess is as good as mine.

at a crossroads.meaning: at a point where an important decision must be made
at a crossroads.meaning_ja: 岐路に立っている、重大な決断の時
at a crossroads.explanation: A crossroads is where paths diverge—you must choose which direction to go. Means facing a critical decision.
examples: She's at a crossroads in her career.
The country is at a crossroads.

behind the scenes.meaning: out of public view; in secret
behind the scenes.explanation: From theater—what happens backstage is not seen by the audience. Means things happening privately.
examples: A lot of work goes on behind the scenes.
Behind the scenes, they were negotiating a deal.

blow your own trumpet.meaning: to boast about your own achievements
blow your own trumpet.meaning_ja: 自画自賛する、自慢する
blow your own trumpet.explanation: Playing a fanfare for yourself rather than having someone else announce your achievements.
examples: I don't like to blow my own trumpet, but I did win the award.
She's always blowing her own trumpet.

bread and butter.meaning: one's main source of income or livelihood
bread and butter.explanation: Bread and butter are basic sustenance. Your 'bread and butter' is what provides your basic living.
examples: Teaching is my bread and butter.
Small businesses are the bread and butter of the economy.

bridge the gap.meaning: to reduce the difference between two things
bridge the gap.meaning_ja: 隔たりを埋める、差を縮める
bridge the gap.explanation: Building a bridge over a gap connects two separated sides. Means reducing differences or disparities.
examples: We need to bridge the gap between theory and practice.
Technology can help bridge the gap in education.

bring home the bacon.meaning: to earn money for the family
bring home the bacon.meaning_ja: 一家の稼ぎ手となる、生活費を稼ぐ
bring home the bacon.explanation: Possibly from the Dunmow Flitch tradition or county fairs where catching a greased pig won you bacon.
examples: She works two jobs to bring home the bacon.
Someone's got to bring home the bacon.

buckle down.meaning: to start working hard with determination
buckle down.meaning_ja: 本腰を入れる、気を引き締めてやる
buckle down.explanation: Like buckling armor or a belt tight before effort. Means getting serious about work.
examples: It's time to buckle down and study.
We need to buckle down if we're going to meet the deadline.

by all means.explanation: Using all possible means—complete and enthusiastic permission or agreement.
examples: By all means, take the day off.
'May I use your phone?' 'By all means.'

call it quits.meaning: to stop doing something; to end a relationship or activity
call it quits.explanation: Declaring that you're done—quitting an activity or ending a partnership.
examples: After 20 years in business, they decided to call it quits.
The couple finally called it quits.

come hell or high water.meaning: no matter what obstacles arise; regardless of difficulty
come hell or high water.meaning_ja: 何があっても、どんな困難があっても
come hell or high water.explanation: Even if hell breaks loose or floods come, the determination remains. Means absolute resolve.
examples: Come hell or high water, I'll be there.
We'll finish this project, come hell or high water.

come to terms with.meaning: to accept something difficult or unpleasant
come to terms with.meaning_ja: 折り合いをつける、受け入れる
come to terms with.explanation: Reaching an agreement or peace with a difficult reality. Means accepting what cannot be changed.
examples: She's still coming to terms with the diagnosis.
It took time to come to terms with the loss.

cut someone some slack.meaning: to not judge someone too harshly; to be lenient
cut someone some slack.meaning_ja: 大目に見る、多少の余裕を与える
cut someone some slack.explanation: From sailing—giving slack (looseness) in a rope. Means being less strict or demanding with someone.
examples: Cut him some slack—he's new at this.
Could you cut me some slack? I've had a rough day.

cut the mustard.meaning: to meet expectations; to be good enough
cut the mustard.meaning_ja: 基準に達する、十分な能力がある
cut the mustard.explanation: Origin debated. Means being able to perform adequately. Usually used in the negative.
examples: The new software just doesn't cut the mustard.
If you can't cut the mustard, we'll find someone who can.

dig deep.meaning: to find extra effort or resources from within yourself
dig deep.meaning_ja: 力を振り絞る、全力を出す
dig deep.explanation: Digging deep into yourself for reserves of strength, courage, or money.
examples: The team had to dig deep to win in overtime.
We all need to dig deep to get through this.

donkey's years.explanation: Possibly from donkey's ears (which are long) punning with years. Means an extremely long period.
examples: I haven't seen him in donkey's years.
She's been working there for donkey's years.

drop a bombshell.meaning: to announce shocking or surprising news
drop a bombshell.meaning_ja: 爆弾発言をする、衝撃的な知らせを伝える
drop a bombshell.explanation: A bombshell explosion causes shock and destruction. Means delivering news that shocks everyone.
examples: She dropped a bombshell when she announced her resignation.
The CEO dropped a bombshell at the meeting.

eye-opener.meaning: an experience that reveals something surprising or enlightening
eye-opener.meaning_ja: 目を見開かせるもの、驚くべき発見
eye-opener.explanation: Something that opens your eyes—makes you see things you hadn't noticed before.
examples: Traveling abroad was a real eye-opener.
The documentary was an eye-opener about food waste.

fall short.meaning: to fail to reach a goal or standard
fall short.meaning_ja: 及ばない、不十分である
fall short.explanation: Like an arrow that falls short of the target. Means not quite reaching what was expected.
examples: Sales fell short of expectations.
The team's effort fell short this time.

get out of hand.explanation: A horse getting out of the rider's hand—no longer under control.
examples: The situation quickly got out of hand.
Spending has gotten out of hand.

get wind of.meaning: to learn about something secret or private
get wind of.meaning_ja: 嗅ぎつける、情報を得る
get wind of.explanation: Like an animal detecting a scent on the wind. Means receiving information about something not publicly known.
examples: If the press gets wind of this, we're in trouble.
She got wind of the surprise party.

give and take.meaning: mutual compromise; willingness to make concessions
give and take.explanation: Both sides give something and take something. Means reaching agreement through mutual concession.
examples: Any good relationship requires give and take.
Negotiation is all about give and take.

go belly up.meaning: to fail completely, especially a business
go belly up.explanation: Like a dead fish floating belly up. Means going bankrupt or completely failing.
examples: The startup went belly up after two years.
Several restaurants went belly up during the pandemic.

go out on a limb.meaning: to take a risk; to put yourself in a vulnerable position
go out on a limb.meaning_ja: リスクを冒す、危険を承知で行動する
go out on a limb.explanation: Climbing out on a tree limb is dangerous—it might break. Means taking a risky position.
examples: I'll go out on a limb and predict she'll win.
He went out on a limb to support the unpopular proposal.

go south.meaning_ja: 悪化する、うまくいかなくなる
go south.explanation: On charts and maps, south is downward. When something goes south, it's heading down/declining.
examples: The project went south after the lead developer quit.
Stock prices went south during the crisis.

gut feeling.meaning: an instinctive feeling without logical reasoning
gut feeling.explanation: A feeling that comes from your gut (stomach) rather than your head. An intuitive sense about something.
examples: I have a gut feeling something is wrong.
Trust your gut feeling on this one.

hands down.meaning: without a doubt; clearly and easily
hands down.explanation: From horse racing—winning with hands down (relaxed reins) means winning so easily there's no contest.
examples: She's hands down the best singer in the group.
That was hands down the best meal I've ever had.

hard pill to swallow.meaning_ja: 受け入れがたいこと、苦い薬
hard pill to swallow.explanation: Bitter medicine is hard to swallow. Means an unpleasant truth or reality that must be accepted.
examples: The rejection was a hard pill to swallow.
It's a hard pill to swallow, but we need to cut costs.

have a blast.meaning: to have a very enjoyable time
have a blast.explanation: A 'blast' means an explosion of fun and excitement. Means having an extremely good time.
examples: We had a blast at the concert!
The kids are going to have a blast at the amusement park.

head start.meaning: an advantage gained by starting before others
head start.explanation: Starting ahead of competitors in a race. Means having an early advantage.
examples: Her experience gave her a head start in the new job.
Getting up early gives you a head start on the day.

in a league of your own.meaning_ja: 他とは別格、比較にならないほど優れている
in a league of your own.explanation: So good that you can't be compared to others—you're in a different league entirely.
examples: When it comes to cooking, she's in a league of her own.
That restaurant is in a league of its own.

in full swing.meaning: at the highest level of activity
in full swing.meaning_ja: 本格的に行われている、最盛期で
in full swing.explanation: Like a pendulum at the peak of its swing—maximum motion and energy. Means fully active and operational.
examples: By noon, the party was in full swing.
Construction is in full swing on the new building.

in the long run.meaning: over a long period of time; eventually
in the long run.meaning_ja: 長い目で見れば、結局は
in the long run.explanation: Thinking about the extended future rather than immediate results.
examples: In the long run, education pays off.
This investment will be profitable in the long run.

jump through hoops.meaning: to go through many difficult steps to achieve something
jump through hoops.meaning_ja: 面倒な手続きをこなす、苦労して要件を満たす
jump through hoops.explanation: Like a circus animal jumping through hoops to please the trainer. Means completing excessive requirements.
examples: We had to jump through hoops to get the permit.
They make you jump through hoops just to get a refund.

keep a low profile.meaning_ja: 目立たないようにする、控えめにしている
keep a low profile.explanation: Keeping your profile (outline) low makes you less visible. Means staying out of the spotlight.
examples: After the scandal, he kept a low profile.
It's best to keep a low profile until things settle down.

keep your fingers crossed.meaning: to hope for good luck or a positive outcome
keep your fingers crossed.meaning_ja: 幸運を祈る、うまくいくことを願う
keep your fingers crossed.explanation: Crossing fingers is a gesture believed to bring good luck.
examples: Keep your fingers crossed for the test results.
I'm keeping my fingers crossed that it doesn't rain.

kick the can down the road.meaning: to postpone dealing with a problem
kick the can down the road.explanation: Like a child kicking a can further along the road instead of picking it up. Means delaying action on an issue.
examples: Politicians keep kicking the can down the road on healthcare.
We can't keep kicking the can down the road.

learn the ropes.meaning: to learn the basics of a job or activity
learn the ropes.meaning_ja: 仕事の基本を覚える、要領を学ぶ
learn the ropes.explanation: From sailing—new sailors had to learn which ropes controlled which sails. Means learning the fundamentals.
examples: It takes a few weeks to learn the ropes.
She's still learning the ropes at her new company.

level the playing field.meaning: to make conditions fair and equal for everyone
level the playing field.meaning_ja: 条件を平等にする、公平な場を作る
level the playing field.explanation: An uneven playing field gives one side an advantage. Leveling it means making competition fair.
examples: The new regulations level the playing field for small businesses.
Education can help level the playing field.

live and learn.meaning: you learn from your mistakes and experiences
live and learn.meaning_ja: 失敗から学ぶ、経験から知恵を得る
live and learn.explanation: Life itself is a learning process. Accepting that mistakes are part of growing.
examples: I won't make that mistake again—live and learn.
It was embarrassing, but live and learn.

make waves.meaning_ja: 波紋を起こす、物議を醸す
make waves.explanation: Like disturbing calm water by creating waves. Means causing disruption or controversy.
examples: The new policy is making waves in the industry.
She's not afraid to make waves when she sees injustice.

move mountains.meaning: to achieve something that seems impossible through great effort
move mountains.meaning_ja: 不可能を可能にする、山を動かす
move mountains.explanation: Moving a mountain would require extraordinary effort. Means accomplishing seemingly impossible feats.
examples: With enough determination, you can move mountains.
She would move mountains for her children.

off the hook.meaning_ja: 責任を免れて、罰を逃れて
off the hook.explanation: Like a fish getting off the hook—escaping capture. Means being freed from a difficult situation or punishment.
examples: The charges were dropped, so he's off the hook.
Don't let them off the hook so easily.

on the go.meaning_ja: 忙しく動き回って、移動中で
on the go.explanation: Constantly going somewhere or doing something. Means being in constant motion or activity.
examples: I've been on the go all day.
She's always on the go—I don't know how she does it.

on the verge of.meaning: very close to a particular state or event
on the verge of.meaning_ja: 〜の瀬戸際に、まさに〜しようとしている
on the verge of.explanation: Standing on the very edge (verge) of something. Means being about to experience or do something.
examples: The company is on the verge of bankruptcy.
She was on the verge of tears.

open a can of worms.meaning: to create a set of complicated problems
open a can of worms.meaning_ja: 面倒な問題を引き起こす
open a can of worms.explanation: Once the can is open, the worms escape and are hard to contain. Means starting something problematic.
examples: Investigating this could open a can of worms.
That question opened a whole can of worms.

par for the course.meaning_ja: いつものこと、予想通りのこと
par for the course.explanation: From golf—par is the expected number of strokes. Means something is typical and unsurprising.
examples: Delays are par for the course in construction.
His late arrival was par for the course.

pick up the slack.meaning: to do extra work that someone else is not doing
pick up the slack.meaning_ja: 人の分まで仕事をする、穴を埋める
pick up the slack.explanation: From pulling a slack rope tight. Means compensating for another person's lack of effort.
examples: When he's absent, the rest of us pick up the slack.
Someone needs to pick up the slack while she's on leave.

play your cards right.meaning: to act wisely and make good decisions
play your cards right.meaning_ja: うまく立ち回る、カードをうまく使う
play your cards right.explanation: From card games—playing strategically leads to winning. Means making smart choices.
examples: If you play your cards right, you could get the promotion.
Play your cards right and this could be a great opportunity.

raise eyebrows.meaning: to cause surprise or mild disapproval
raise eyebrows.meaning_ja: 驚かせる、眉をひそめさせる
raise eyebrows.explanation: People raise their eyebrows when surprised or skeptical. Means causing a reaction of surprise.
examples: Her unconventional dress raised a few eyebrows.
The CEO's salary raised eyebrows.

right off the bat.explanation: From baseball—right when the bat hits the ball. Means at the very start, without delay.
examples: Right off the bat, I could tell something was wrong.
He impressed everyone right off the bat.

roll up your sleeves.meaning_ja: 腕まくりをする、本腰を入れる
roll up your sleeves.explanation: Literally rolling up sleeves to prevent them from getting dirty during manual work. Means getting ready for hard work.
examples: It's time to roll up our sleeves and get to work.
She rolled up her sleeves and started fixing the code.

run of the mill.explanation: From manufacturing—products that come off the mill without special treatment. Means average and unremarkable.
examples: It was a run-of-the-mill performance, nothing special.
This isn't a run-of-the-mill restaurant—it's exceptional.

safe and sound.explanation: Both safe (free from danger) and sound (in good condition). A reassuring expression about someone's wellbeing.
examples: The children arrived home safe and sound.
All the passengers were safe and sound after the emergency landing.

set in stone.meaning_ja: 確定した、変更不可能な
set in stone.explanation: Words carved in stone cannot be easily altered. Means something is final and permanent.
examples: The schedule isn't set in stone—we can adjust it.
Once the contract is signed, it's set in stone.

sink or swim.meaning: to either fail or succeed by your own efforts
sink or swim.meaning_ja: 自力でなんとかするしかない
sink or swim.explanation: Thrown into water, you either learn to swim or you sink. Means being left to succeed or fail on your own.
examples: In this job, it's sink or swim from day one.
They threw me into the project—sink or swim.

sitting duck.meaning: an easy target; someone vulnerable to attack
sitting duck.meaning_ja: 格好の餌食、無防備な状態
sitting duck.explanation: A duck sitting still on water is an easy target for hunters. Means being vulnerable and defenseless.
examples: Without security, the data is a sitting duck for hackers.
Our competitor is a sitting duck right now.

the tip of the iceberg.meaning: only a small, visible part of a much larger issue
the tip of the iceberg.explanation: Most of an iceberg is hidden underwater. What you see is only a fraction of the whole problem.
examples: These reported cases are just the tip of the iceberg.
What we've found so far is only the tip of the iceberg.

third wheel.meaning: an unwanted extra person, especially with a couple
third wheel.explanation: A third wheel on a bicycle is unnecessary and unwanted. Means being an awkward extra person.
examples: I felt like a third wheel on their date.
I don't want to be a third wheel—you two go ahead.

tight-lipped.explanation: Lips pressed tightly together, refusing to speak. Means keeping silent about something.
examples: The company was tight-lipped about the investigation.
He remained tight-lipped about his plans.

up and running.explanation: A machine or system that has been started and is working as intended.
examples: The new server is up and running.
We'll have the system up and running by Monday.

walk the walk.meaning: to actually do what you say you will do
walk the walk.meaning_ja: 言行一致する、有言実行する
walk the walk.explanation: From 'talk the talk and walk the walk.' Talking is promising; walking is delivering on those promises.
examples: It's easy to talk the talk, but can you walk the walk?
The company doesn't just talk about sustainability—they walk the walk.

with flying colors.explanation: From naval tradition—ships returning from battle with flags (colors) still flying high meant victory.
examples: She passed the exam with flying colors.
The product launch went off with flying colors.

work against the clock.meaning: to work under severe time pressure
work against the clock.explanation: Racing against time itself. Means working urgently because a deadline is approaching.
examples: The team is working against the clock to fix the bug.
We're working against the clock to meet the deadline.

back to basics.meaning_ja: 基本に立ち返る、原点回帰
back to basics.explanation: When things get too complicated, going back to the simple fundamentals can help. Means refocusing on core principles.
examples: The team needs to go back to basics.
Sometimes the best strategy is back to basics.

turn the tables.meaning: to reverse a situation to gain an advantage
turn the tables.meaning_ja: 形勢を逆転する、立場を入れ替える
turn the tables.explanation: From board games where turning the table around puts your opponent in your position. Means reversing who has the advantage.
examples: The underdog turned the tables in the second half.
She turned the tables on her critics by succeeding.

cut to the quick.explanation: The 'quick' is the sensitive flesh under fingernails. Cutting to it means causing deep emotional pain.
examples: His harsh words cut her to the quick.
The betrayal cut him to the quick.

a penny saved is a penny earned.meaning: saving money is just as valuable as earning it
a penny saved is a penny earned.meaning_ja: 節約は稼ぐのと同じ価値がある
a penny saved is a penny earned.explanation: Attributed to Benjamin Franklin. Emphasizes the importance of frugality.
examples: I always look for discounts—a penny saved is a penny earned.
Why pay full price? A penny saved is a penny earned.

two cents.explanation: Offering your 'two cents' means sharing your opinion, implying it may not be worth much.
examples: If I can put in my two cents, I think we should wait.
Nobody asked for his two cents, but he gave it anyway.

in a rut.meaning: stuck in a boring, unchanging pattern
in a rut.meaning_ja: マンネリに陥っている
in a rut.explanation: A rut is a groove worn in the ground by wheels. Being in one means following the same track without variation.
examples: I feel like I'm in a rut at work.
If you're in a rut, try something new.

the whole shebang.explanation: Origin debated—possibly from 'shebeen' (an unlicensed bar). Means the complete package.
examples: We redecorated the house—new paint, furniture, the whole shebang.
They catered the event with appetizers, main course, dessert—the whole shebang.

take one for the team.meaning: to sacrifice your own interests for the group's benefit
take one for the team.meaning_ja: チームのために犠牲を払う
take one for the team.explanation: From sports—enduring something painful (like getting hit by a pitch) for the team's advantage.
examples: Someone has to work Saturday—I'll take one for the team.
She took one for the team by handling the difficult client.
